SOP AUTHORING GUIDE (the comprehensive prompt) ===\n\nauthoring_guide = r'''# SOP Authoring Guide\n## Standard Operating Procedure — Creation & Management Manual\n\n| Field | Detail |\n|---|---|\n| **Document ID** | SOP-META-001 |\n| **Purpose** | Standardized creation of trade SOPs |\n| **Revision** | 1.0 |\n| **Effective Date** | 2026-02-28 |\n\n---\n\n## 1. Purpose\n\nThis guide defines the standardized format, structure, and conventions for authoring Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) across all trades. It ensures consistency, cross-referenceability, and usability for field workers, supervisors, and digital work-order platforms.\n\n---\n\n## 2. SOP Numbering Convention\n\n### Format: `{TRADE}-SOP-{NNN}`\n\n| Code | Trade | Example |\n|---|---|---|\n| PLB | Plumbing | PLB-SOP-001 |\n| ELC | Electrical | ELC-SOP-001 |\n| HVC | HVAC | HVC-SOP-001 |\n| CRP | Carpentry | CRP-SOP-001 |\n| PNT | Painting | PNT-SOP-001 |\n| RFG | Roofing | RFG-SOP-001 |\n| FLR | Flooring | FLR-SOP-001 |\n| DRW | Drywall | DRW-SOP-001 |\n| LND | Landscaping | LND-SOP-001 |\n| GEN | General / Multi-trade | GEN-SOP-001 |\n| MSN | Masonry | MSN-SOP-001 |\n| WLD | Welding | WLD-SOP-001 |\n\nAdd new trade codes to the SOP Library Index (`SOP_INDEX.md`) as needed.\n\n---\n\n## 3. Mandatory Section Structure\n\nEvery SOP **must** contain the following sections in this order. Sections may be omitted only if explicitly marked N/A with justification.\n\n```\n# Standard Operating Procedure\n## {Trade} — {Task Name}\n\n| Header Table: SOP Number, Trade, Task, Revision, Date, Approved By, Est. Duration |\n\n## 1. Purpose\n## 2. Scope\n## 3. References & Codes\n## 4. Personnel & Qualifications\n## 5. Safety Requirements\n   ### 5.1 Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)\n   ### 5.2 Hazard Awareness (table format)\n   ### 5.3 Pre-Work Safety Checklist\n## 6. Tools & Equipment\n   ### 6.1 Required Tools (table format)\n   ### 6.2 Required Materials (table format)\n## 7. Pre-Work Assessment\n## 8–N. Procedure Sections (one per phase — see Phase Rules below)\n## N+1. Testing & Commissioning\n## N+2. Cleanup & Closeout\n## N+3. Troubleshooting (table format)\n## N+4. Quality Standards\n## N+5. Revision History (table format)\n```\n\n---\n\n## 4. Procedure Phase Rules\n\nEach procedure phase (e.g., Removal, Installation, Commissioning) gets its own numbered section and follows this **exact internal structure**:\n\n```\n## {N}. Procedure — {Phase Name}\n\n### Executive Checklist\n\nComplete each step in order. Check off the step when **all tasks within it are finished**.\n\n| Step | Step Name | Tasks | ✓ |\n|:---:|---|---|:---:|\n| **1** | **{Step Name}** | {Brief task description} | ☐ |\n| **2** | **{Step Name}** | {Brief task description} | ☐ |\n| ... | ... | ... | ☐ |\n\n> ✅ **All steps complete?** → Proceed to Section {next} — {next section name}.\n\n---\n\n### Detailed Steps\n\n#### Step 1: {Step Name}\n{Numbered instructions}\n\n#### Step 2: {Step Name}\n{Numbered instructions}\n\n...\n```\n\n### Rules:\n\n1. **Maximum 5 steps per Executive Checklist** — group related tasks to stay within this limit\n2. **Step Names must match exactly** between the Executive Checklist table and the Detailed Steps headings\n3. **Tasks column** contains a brief, action-oriented summary (one line)\n4. **Detailed Steps** use `####` heading level with the format `#### Step N: {Step Name}`\n5. **Sub-groupings** within a step use bold text labels (e.g., `**Supply lines:**`, `**Drain assembly:**`)\n6. **Numbered lists** for sequential actions; **bullet lists** for non-sequential items\n7. **Warnings/code checks** use blockquote format: `> ⚠️ **Code Check:** ...`\n8. **Best practices** use blockquote format: `> **Best practice:** ...`\n\n---\n\n## 5. Linking & Nesting SOPs\n\nSOPs frequently reference other SOPs. Use these conventions for maintainability and traceability.\n\n### 5.1 Link Types\n\n| Link Type | When to Use | Format |\n|---|---|---|\n| **Cross-reference** | Task is covered by a separate SOP | `(see {TRADE}-SOP-{NNN}: {Title})` |\n| **Prerequisite** | Another SOP must be completed first | `**Prerequisite:** Complete {TRADE}-SOP-{NNN} before proceeding.` |\n| **Nested / Sub-procedure** | A step within this SOP invokes another SOP entirely | `**Sub-procedure:** Execute {TRADE}-SOP-{NNN}: {Title} at this point.` |\n| **Optional add-on** | Another SOP may apply depending on scope | `**If applicable:** See {TRADE}-SOP-{NNN}: {Title}` |\n| **Supersedes** | This SOP replaces an older version or different SOP | `**Supersedes:** {TRADE}-SOP-{NNN} Rev {X}` |\n\n### 5.2 Where to Place Links\n\n| Location | Link Types Allowed |\n|---|---|\n| **Section 2 (Scope)** | Cross-references for excluded tasks, optional add-ons |\n| **Section 7 (Pre-Work)** | Prerequisites |\n| **Procedure Steps** | Nested/sub-procedures, cross-references |\n| **Header Table** | Supersedes |\n| **SOP Library Index** | All — master cross-reference |\n\n### 5.3 Nesting Rules\n\n1. **Maximum nesting depth: 2 levels** — An SOP can call a sub-procedure SOP, but that sub-procedure should NOT call another sub-procedure. If it does, refactor.\n2. **Always specify return point** — After a nested SOP, state: `Return to Step {N} of this procedure after completing {TRADE}-SOP-{NNN}.`\n3. **Scope boundaries** — Clearly state in Section 2 what is handled by linked SOPs vs. this SOP\n4. **Circular references prohibited** — SOP-A cannot reference SOP-B if SOP-B references SOP-A at the same nesting level\n\n### 5.4 Linking Example\n\nIn a kitchen sink replacement SOP:\n\n```markdown\n## 2. Scope\n\nThis SOP covers:\n- Disconnection and removal of the existing sink\n- Installation of the new sink, faucet, and drain assembly\n\nThis SOP does **not** cover:\n- Garbage disposal installation (see PLB-SOP-005: Garbage Disposal Install)\n- Dishwasher connection (see PLB-SOP-006: Dishwasher Hookup)\n- Relocation of supply or drain rough-in (see PLB-SOP-010: Rough-In Relocation)\n```\n\nIn a procedure step:\n\n```markdown\n#### Step 5: Reconnect Ancillaries & Prep for Test\n\n- **If garbage disposal is in scope:**\n  **Sub-procedure:** Execute PLB-SOP-005: Garbage Disposal Install at this point.\n  Return to Step 5 of this procedure after completing PLB-SOP-005.\n\n- Dishwasher drain hose to tailpiece air gap\n- Filtered water line\n```\n\n### 5.5 Dependency Diagram (for complex jobs)\n\nFor work orders involving 3+ SOPs, include a dependency diagram in the work order documentation:\n\n```\n┌─────────────────────┐\n│ PLB-SOP-001          │\n│ Sink Replacement     │\n├─────────────────────┤\n│ Step 5 ──────────────┼──► PLB-SOP-005: Garbage Disposal\n│ Step 5 ──────────────┼──► PLB-SOP-006: Dishwasher Hookup\n└─────────────────────┘\n         │\n         ▼\n┌─────────────────────┐\n│ PLB-SOP-020          │\n│ Final Inspection     │\n└─────────────────────┘\n```\n\n---\n\n## 6. SOP Library Index\n\nYes — **a library index table is required**. Maintain it in `SOP_INDEX.md` in the same directory as the SOPs.\n\nThe index serves as:\n- **Master catalog** of all SOPs in the system\n- **Cross-reference map** showing dependencies between SOPs\n- **Version tracker** for revision management\n- **Search aid** for field workers and supervisors\n- **Work order integration** — work orders reference SOP IDs from this index\n\nSee `SOP_INDEX.md` for the live index.\n\n---\n\n## 7. AI Prompt for SOP Generation\n\nUse the following prompt to generate new SOPs in the standardized format. Replace all `{placeholders}` with actual values.\n\n---\n\n### Prompt Template\n\n```\nCreate a Standard Operating Procedure in Markdown format following these exact specifications:\n\n**TASK DEFINITION:**\n- Trade: {trade name}\n- Trade Code: {3-letter code from numbering convention}\n- SOP Number: {TRADE}-SOP-{NNN}\n- Task: {specific task description}\n- Estimated Duration: {time range}\n\n**FORMAT REQUIREMENTS:**\n\n1. **Header Table** with fields: SOP Number, Trade, Task, Revision (1.0), Effective Date, Approved By (blank), Estimated Duration\n\n2. **Mandatory Sections** in this order:\n   - Purpose\n   - Scope (include what IS and IS NOT covered; cross-reference related SOPs using format: `(see {CODE}-SOP-{NNN}: {Title})`)\n   - References & Codes (applicable building codes, OSHA standards, manufacturer specs)\n   - Personnel & Qualifications (table: Role, Requirement)\n   - Safety Requirements:\n     - 5.1 PPE list\n     - 5.2 Hazard Awareness table (columns: Hazard, Mitigation)\n     - 5.3 Pre-Work Safety Checklist (checkbox format)\n   - Tools & Equipment:\n     - 6.1 Required Tools table (columns: Tool, Purpose)\n     - 6.2 Required Materials table (columns: Material, Specification)\n   - Pre-Work Assessment (site inspection, customer communication)\n\n3. **Procedure Sections** — one per phase (e.g., Removal, Installation). Each MUST follow this structure:\n\n   ```\n   ## {N}. Procedure — {Phase Name}\n\n   ### Executive Checklist\n   | Step | Step Name | Tasks | ✓ |\n   |:---:|---|---|:---:|\n   | **1** | **{Name}** | {Brief description} | ☐ |\n   (maximum 5 steps per checklist)\n\n   ### Detailed Steps\n   #### Step 1: {Same Step Name as Executive Checklist}\n   {numbered instructions}\n   ```\n\n   Rules:\n   - Maximum 5 steps per Executive Checklist\n   - Step Names MUST match exactly between Executive Checklist and Detailed Steps headings\n   - Use bold labels for sub-groupings within steps\n   - Use `> ⚠️` for code checks and warnings\n   - Use `> **Best practice:**` for recommendations\n\n4. **Testing & Commissioning** — staged testing with checkbox format\n\n5. **Cleanup & Closeout** — site cleanup, documentation, customer handoff\n\n6. **Troubleshooting** table (columns: Issue, Probable Cause, Resolution)\n\n7. **Quality Standards** — code compliance, workmanship standards\n\n8. **Revision History** table (columns: Rev, Date, Author, Description)\n\n**LINKING RULES:**\n- Cross-reference: `(see {CODE}-SOP-{NNN}: {Title})`\n- Prerequisite: `**Prerequisite:** Complete {CODE}-SOP-{NNN} before proceeding.`\n- Sub-procedure: `**Sub-procedure:** Execute {CODE}-SOP-{NNN}: {Title} at this point. Return to Step {N} after completing.`\n- Optional: `**If applicable:** See {CODE}-SOP-{NNN}: {Title}`\n\n**RELATED SOPs** (for cross-referencing in Scope section):\n{list any related SOPs that exist in the library}\n\n**ADDITIONAL CONTEXT:**\n{any trade-specific details, code requirements, or special considerations}\n```\n\n---\n\n## 8. Quality Checklist for SOP Authors\n\nBefore publishing any SOP, verify:\n\n- [ ] SOP Number follows `{TRADE}-SOP-{NNN}` convention\n- [ ] All mandatory sections present in correct order\n- [ ] Each procedure phase has Executive Checklist + Detailed Steps\n- [ ] Executive Checklists have ≤ 5 steps each\n- [ ] Step Names match exactly between Executive Checklist and Detailed Steps\n- [ ] All cross-references use correct format and point to valid SOPs\n- [ ] No circular references between SOPs\n- [ ] Nesting depth ≤ 2 levels\n- [ ] Safety section includes PPE, Hazard table, and Pre-Work checklist\n- [ ] Tools and Materials tables are complete with specifications\n- [ ] Testing section has staged verification steps\n- [ ] Troubleshooting table covers common failure modes\n- [ ] Revision History table is current\n- [ ] SOP added to `SOP_INDEX.md` library\n- [ ] File saved as `SOP_{Trade}_{Task_Name}.md` (underscores, no spaces)\n\n---\n\n## 9. Revision History\n\n| Rev | Date | Author | Description |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| 1.0 | 2026-02-28 | — | Initial release |\n\n---\n\n*This guide is the authoritative reference for SOP creation. All SOPs must conform to this standard.*\n'''\n\n# === 2. Scope\n\nThis SOP covers:\n- De-energizing the circuit and verifying zero-energy state\n- Removal of the existing receptacle, cover plate, and wiring connections\n- Installation of the replacement receptacle with proper wiring terminations\n- Testing for correct wiring, grounding, and GFCI protection\n- Cover plate installation and final commissioning\n\nThis SOP does **not** cover:\n- New circuit installation or home run wiring (see ELC-SOP-005: New Branch Circuit Installation)\n- Electrical panel upgrades or breaker replacement (see ELC-SOP-010: Panel & Breaker Service)\n- Whole-room rewiring or knob-and-tube replacement (see ELC-SOP-015: Legacy Wiring Remediation)\n- Dedicated 240V circuit installation for appliances (see ELC-SOP-020: Dedicated Appliance Circuit)\n- Smart outlet / home automation integration (see ELC-SOP-025: Smart Device Installation)\n- Outdoor / wet-location receptacle installation requiring new weatherproof boxes (see ELC-SOP-030: Exterior Receptacle Installation)\n\n---\n\n## 3. Safety Requirements\n\n### 5.1 Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)\n- Safety glasses / goggles\n- Insulated electrical gloves (rated for voltage class — minimum Class 00 for 120V)\n- Leather protector gloves over insulated gloves\n- Non-conductive / dielectric footwear\n- Arc-rated clothing (if panel work required — minimum 8 cal/cm²)\n- Hearing protection (if using power tools)\n\n### 5.2 Hazard Awareness\n| Hazard | Mitigation |\n|---|---|\n| Electrical shock (120V/240V) | LOTO procedure; verify zero-energy with rated tester; never trust a single test |\n| Arc flash | Wear arc-rated PPE; maintain safe approach boundaries per NFPA 70E |\n| Fire risk | Verify wire gauge matches breaker rating; ensure tight connections; no backstab on 20A circuits |\n| Damaged / deteriorated wiring | Inspect all visible wiring; report and remediate before proceeding |\n| Asbestos (older homes) | If suspected in wall material, stop work and report; do not disturb |\n| Aluminum wiring (pre-1976 homes) | Identify by color (silver vs. copper); use CO/ALR rated devices only; apply anti-oxidant compound |\n| Confined space / awkward position | Use proper body mechanics; ensure adequate lighting |\n| Existing moisture / water damage | Test for moisture before energizing; install GFCI if wet location |\n\n### 5.3 Pre-Work Safety Checklist\n- [ ] Circuit identified and breaker labeled at panel\n- [ ] Breaker turned OFF at electrical panel\n- [ ] Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) applied to breaker\n- [ ] Zero-energy state verified with **rated** voltage tester (CAT III minimum)\n- [ ] Voltage tester verified on known live source before AND after testing circuit\n- [ ] Work area cleared and dry\n- [ ] Non-contact voltage tester (NCVT) available as secondary check\n- [ ] Other occupants / trades notified of de-energized circuit\n- [ ] Fire extinguisher accessible (Class C rated for electrical fires)\n- [ ] First aid kit on site\n\n> ⚠️ **CRITICAL SAFETY RULE:** NEVER work on energized circuits. Always verify zero-energy state with a rated voltage tester. Test the tester on a known live source before and after use (Live-Dead-Live method).\n\n---\n\n## 6. Tools & Equipment\n\n### 6.1 Required Tools\n| Tool | Purpose |\n|---|---|\n| Non-contact voltage tester (NCVT) | Initial screening for energized conductors |\n| Rated voltage tester (CAT III/IV) | Definitive zero-energy verification (Live-Dead-Live) |\n| Receptacle / circuit tester (3-light or digital) | Post-install wiring verification |\n| GFCI tester | Verify GFCI trip/reset function |\n| Insulated screwdrivers (Phillips #2, flat 1/4\") | Terminal screws, cover plate, device mounting |\n| Wire strippers (14–10 AWG) | Preparing conductor ends |\n| Needle-nose pliers (insulated) | Forming wire hooks, positioning conductors |\n| Lineman's pliers (insulated) | Twisting wires, cutting conductors |\n| Utility knife | Scoring paint, trimming sheathing |\n| Flashlight / headlamp | Box interior visibility |\n| Torpedo level | Verify receptacle is plumb |\n| Tape measure | Verify box fill, wire length |\n| Wire nuts / Wago connectors (assorted) | Splicing conductors (if pigtailing) |\n| Electrical tape (black, white, green) | Insulating connections, re-identifying conductors |\n| Permanent marker | Labeling wires if multiple cables in box |\n\n### 6.2 Required Materials\n| Material | Specification |\n|---|---|\n| Replacement receptacle | Per work order — match amperage (15A/20A), voltage (120V/240V), type (duplex/GFCI/USB/decorator) |\n| Cover plate | Match receptacle style (standard/decorator/weatherproof) |\n| Wire nuts or Wago lever connectors | Sized for conductor count and gauge |\n| Pigtail wires (if needed) | Same gauge as circuit (14 AWG for 15A, 12 AWG for 20A) — 6\" minimum |\n| Anti-oxidant compound | **Required** if aluminum wiring is present |\n| Electrical tape | UL-listed, 600V rated |\n| Box fill spacers / extension ring | If existing box is undersized or recessed |\n| Device mounting screws | #6-32 machine screws (if originals are damaged) |\n\n---\n\n## 7. Pre-Work Assessment\n\n### 7.1 Site Assessment\n1. **Verify work order** — Confirm receptacle type, amperage, location, and any special requirements (GFCI, tamper-resistant, weather-resistant, USB)\n2. **Identify the circuit:**\n   - Locate the correct breaker at the electrical panel\n   - Verify breaker amperage matches receptacle rating (15A breaker → 15A or 15A receptacle; 20A breaker → 20A receptacle)\n   - Note wire gauge visible at panel (14 AWG = 15A max; 12 AWG = 20A max; 10 AWG = 30A max)\n3. **Assess existing conditions:**\n   - Age of home and wiring type (copper, aluminum, knob-and-tube)\n   - Condition of existing receptacle and cover plate\n   - Box type and condition (metal vs. plastic; old work vs. new work)\n   - Number of cables entering the box (affects box fill calculation)\n   - Presence of ground wire\n4. **NEC location requirements:**\n   - Kitchen countertop → 20A GFCI required\n   - Bathroom → 20A GFCI required\n   - Garage → GFCI required\n   - Outdoor → GFCI + weather-resistant (WR) + in-use cover required\n   - Laundry → 20A GFCI required (NEC 2023+)\n   - Within 6' of water source → GFCI required\n   - Anywhere accessible to children → Tamper-resistant (TR) required (all new/replacement)\n5. **Photograph existing installation** for documentation\n6. **Stage all tools and materials** in work area\n\n### 7.2 Customer Communication\n- Confirm scope of work and receptacle selection\n- Advise which circuits will be de-energized and estimated duration\n- Identify any devices on the same circuit that will lose power (refrigerator, medical equipment, computers)\n- Discuss any discovered issues (aluminum wiring, missing ground, undersized box) and get authorization before additional work\n- Advise if permit and inspection are required per local jurisdiction\n\n---\n\n## 8. Procedure — Removal of Existing Receptacle\n\n### Executive Checklist\n\nComplete each step in order. Check off the step when **all tasks within it are finished**.\n\n| Step | Step Name | Tasks | ✓ |\n|:---:|---|---|:---:|\n| **1** | **Isolate & Verify Zero Energy** | Turn off breaker, apply LOTO, verify dead with rated tester (Live-Dead-Live) | ☐ |\n| **2** | **Remove Cover & Device** | Remove cover plate, unscrew device from box, pull receptacle out carefully | ☐ |\n| **3** | **Document & Disconnect Wiring** | Photograph wiring, label wires, disconnect all conductors from terminals | ☐ |\n| **4** | **Inspect Box & Wiring** | Check box condition, wire condition, box fill, grounding, and wiring type | ☐ |\n| **5** | **Prepare for Installation** | Straighten conductors, strip wire ends if needed, clean box, resolve any issues found | ☐ |\n\n> ✅ **All 5 steps complete?** → Proceed to Section 9 — Installation.\n\n---\n\n### Detailed Steps\n\n#### Step 1: Isolate & Verify Zero Energy\n1. Identify the correct breaker at the electrical panel\n2. Turn breaker to the OFF position\n3. Apply lockout/tagout (LOTO) device to breaker — attach personal lock and tag\n4. Return to the receptacle location\n5. **Verify voltage tester on a known live source** (confirm tester is working)\n6. Test between hot and neutral, hot and ground, and neutral and ground at the receptacle\n7. Confirm **zero voltage on all combinations**\n8. **Verify voltage tester again on a known live source** (Live-Dead-Live complete)\n9. Use non-contact voltage tester (NCVT) as secondary confirmation\n\n> ⚠️ **CRITICAL:** Never skip the Live-Dead-Live verification. A tester with dead batteries gives a false \"safe\" reading. Two independent tests (rated tester + NCVT) provide defense-in-depth.\n\n#### Step 2: Remove Cover & Device\n1. Remove cover plate screw(s) — set aside\n2. Remove cover plate — note condition (replace if cracked, painted over, or discolored)\n3. Remove the two device mounting screws (top and bottom #6-32)\n4. Gently pull the receptacle straight out of the box\n5. **Do not yank** — wires may be short or brittle in older installations\n6. Pull out enough slack to work comfortably (minimum 6\" from box face per NEC 300.14)\n\n#### Step 3: Document & Disconnect Wiring\n1. **Photograph the wiring configuration** before disconnecting anything\n2. **Count and label all wires** with tape or marker:\n   - HOT (black or red) — brass-colored screw terminal\n   - NEUTRAL (white) — silver-colored screw terminal\n   - GROUND (bare copper or green) — green screw terminal\n   - If multiple cables: label LINE vs. LOAD (critical for GFCI installations)\n3. **Disconnect wires from terminals:**\n\n   **For screw terminals:**\n   - Loosen terminal screw counterclockwise\n   - Unhook wire from under screw head\n\n   **For backstab (push-in) connections:**\n   - Insert small flat screwdriver into release slot next to wire\n   - Pull wire straight out while pressing release\n   - **Note:** Backstab connections are a common failure point — upgrade to screw terminals or screw-to-clamp on reinstallation\n\n4. If wires are daisy-chained (using both sets of terminal screws), note which cable is LINE (from panel) and which is LOAD (continuing to next outlet)\n\n#### Step 4: Inspect Box & Wiring\n1. **Inspect wire condition:**\n   - Look for nicked insulation, scorching, melting, or discoloration\n   - Check for brittle insulation (common in pre-1970 wiring)\n   - Identify wire gauge: 14 AWG (15A circuit) or 12 AWG (20A circuit)\n   - **Identify wiring material:** Copper (orange/salmon color) vs. Aluminum (silver color)\n2. **Inspect box condition:**\n   - Check for cracks, rust, or damage\n   - Verify box is securely mounted\n   - Check box fill capacity (NEC 314.16) — count conductors, clamps, devices\n   - Verify box is flush with wall surface (or within 1/4\" if combustible wall per NEC 314.20)\n3. **Verify grounding:**\n   - Ground wire present and properly connected to box (if metal box)\n   - If no ground wire: determine if metal conduit provides ground path, or if upgrade is needed\n4. **Report any issues found:**\n   - Aluminum wiring → requires CO/ALR rated device + anti-oxidant compound\n   - No ground → discuss options with customer (GFCI protection, rewiring, or ground retrofit)\n   - Undersized box → install extension ring or replace box\n   - Damaged wiring → repair or replace affected section\n\n> ⚠️ **Code Check:** Per NEC 406.4(D), replacement receptacles at locations requiring GFCI protection must be GFCI type, even if the original was not. Per NEC 406.4(C), all replacement receptacles must be tamper-resistant (TR) type.\n\n#### Step 5: Prepare for Installation\n1. Straighten all conductors — avoid excessive bending (especially aluminum)\n2. If wire ends are nicked, scorched, or oxidized: cut back and re-strip\n   - Strip 3/4\" of insulation for screw terminals\n   - Strip per manufacturer spec for screw-to-clamp terminals\n3. If pigtailing is needed (recommended for daisy-chain configurations):\n   - Cut pigtail wires to 6\"–8\" length, same gauge as circuit\n   - Strip ends to match terminal requirements\n4. Clean any debris from the electrical box\n5. If box is recessed: install box extension ring to bring flush with wall\n6. Confirm all issues from Step 4 are resolved or authorized before proceeding\n\n---\n\n## 9. Procedure — Installation of New Receptacle\n\n### Executive Checklist\n\nComplete each step in order. Check off the step when **all tasks within it are finished**.\n\n| Step | Step Name | Tasks | ✓ |\n|:---:|---|---|:---:|\n| **1** | **Prepare & Wire the Receptacle** | Verify device rating, form wire hooks or pigtails, terminate all conductors to correct terminals | ☐ |\n| **2** | **Secure Device in Box** | Fold wires carefully, seat receptacle, tighten mounting screws, verify plumb | ☐ |\n| **3** | **Install Cover Plate** | Attach cover plate, verify flush fit, no gaps | ☐ |\n| **4** | **Energize & Test** | Remove LOTO, energize circuit, test with receptacle tester and voltage tester | ☐ |\n| **5** | **Commission & Document** | Verify GFCI function (if applicable), label circuit, complete documentation | ☐ |\n\n> ✅ **All 5 steps complete?** → Proceed to Section 10 — Testing & Commissioning.\n\n---\n\n### Detailed Steps\n\n#### Step 1: Prepare & Wire the Receptacle\n\n**Verify device before wiring:**\n1. Confirm replacement receptacle matches requirements:\n   - Amperage: 15A or 20A (must match or be compatible with circuit)\n   - Voltage: 120V or 240V\n   - Type: Standard duplex, GFCI, decorator, tamper-resistant (TR), weather-resistant (WR)\n   - Rating: UL listed; CO/ALR if aluminum wiring\n2. Inspect new device for shipping damage\n\n**Wire termination — standard duplex receptacle:**\n1. Form a clockwise hook (\"shepherd's hook\") on each conductor end using needle-nose pliers\n2. Connect **GROUND** (bare/green) to the green screw terminal first\n   - If metal box: also bond ground to box with ground screw or clip\n3. Connect **NEUTRAL** (white) to the silver screw terminal\n   - Hook wraps clockwise under screw head\n   - Tighten screw firmly — wire should not pull free with gentle tug\n4. Connect **HOT** (black) to the brass screw terminal\n   - Hook wraps clockwise under screw head\n   - Tighten screw firmly\n5. If daisy-chaining (multiple cables): use pigtails to a single connection point rather than using both sets of device terminals\n\n**Wire termination — GFCI receptacle:**\n1. Identify LINE cable (from panel) and LOAD cable (to downstream outlets)\n2. Connect LINE hot (black) to **LINE brass** terminal (marked on device)\n3. Connect LINE neutral (white) to **LINE silver** terminal\n4. Connect LOAD hot (black) to **LOAD brass** terminal (if protecting downstream)\n5. Connect LOAD neutral (white) to **LOAD silver** terminal\n6. Connect ground to green screw\n7. If no downstream protection needed: cap LOAD terminals with provided stickers or leave LOAD terminals unused\n\n> ⚠️ **CRITICAL:** Reversing LINE and LOAD on a GFCI will prevent it from protecting downstream outlets and may prevent the device from functioning. Always verify with manufacturer markings.\n\n**Wire termination — 240V receptacle:**\n1. Connect both hot conductors (black and red) to brass terminals\n2. Connect neutral (white) to silver terminal (if 4-wire / NEMA 14)\n3. Connect ground (bare/green) to green terminal\n4. Verify wire gauge matches breaker and receptacle rating\n\n> **Best practice:** Use screw-to-clamp terminals when available — they provide more reliable connections than backstab (push-in) and are faster than hook-and-screw. Never use backstab connections on 20A circuits or with 12 AWG wire.\n\n#### Step 2: Secure Device in Box\n1. Carefully fold wires in an accordion pattern into the box\n   - **Do not force or kink wires** — especially critical with aluminum\n   - Ground wire goes to the back first, then neutral, then hot\n2. Align receptacle mounting ears with box screw holes\n3. Insert and tighten top mounting screw — snug, not overtightened\n4. Insert and tighten bottom mounting screw\n5. Check plumb with torpedo level — adjust using mounting screw slots\n6. Verify receptacle sits flush with wall surface\n7. Verify no wire insulation is pinched between device and box\n\n#### Step 3: Install Cover Plate\n1. Position cover plate over receptacle\n2. Insert cover plate screw(s) — hand-tighten, then snug with screwdriver\n3. **Do not overtighten** — cover plates crack easily (especially nylon/plastic)\n4. Verify cover plate sits flush against wall with no gaps\n5. If wall surface is uneven: use a gasketed or oversized cover plate\n\n#### Step 4: Energize & Test\n1. Return to electrical panel\n2. Remove lockout/tagout device\n3. Turn breaker to ON position\n4. Return to receptacle\n5. **Test with receptacle tester (3-light or digital):**\n   - Verify correct wiring indication (two amber lights for standard 120V)\n   - Check for open ground, open neutral, hot/neutral reverse, hot/ground reverse\n6. **Test with rated voltage tester:**\n   - Hot to neutral: 120V (±5%) for standard; 240V (±5%) for 240V receptacle\n   - Hot to ground: 120V (±5%)\n   - Neutral to ground: <2V (higher indicates neutral/ground issue)\n7. **Test GFCI function (if GFCI receptacle):**\n   - Press TEST button — receptacle should trip (power off)\n   - Press RESET button — receptacle should restore power\n   - Test with external GFCI tester — verify trip time <25ms\n   - Verify downstream outlets also lose power when GFCI trips (if LOAD terminals used)\n\n#### Step 5: Commission & Document\n1. Verify all downstream outlets on the circuit are functioning\n2. Plug in a load (lamp, phone charger) to confirm real-world operation\n3. Label the circuit at the electrical panel if not already labeled\n4. Apply GFCI sticker to downstream outlets protected by upstream GFCI (\"GFCI Protected\" label per NEC 406.4(D))\n5. Complete work order documentation\n\n---\n\n## 10. Testing & Commissioning\n\n### Step 1: Visual Inspection\n- [ ] Receptacle is plumb and flush with wall\n- [ ] Cover plate is secure with no gaps\n- [ ] No visible wire insulation outside the box\n- [ ] Correct receptacle type installed for location (GFCI, TR, WR as required)\n- [ ] Receptacle amperage matches circuit breaker rating\n\n### Step 2: Electrical Test — Receptacle Tester\n- [ ] 3-light / digital tester shows correct wiring\n- [ ] No fault conditions indicated (open ground, reverse polarity, etc.)\n\n### Step 3: Electrical Test — Voltage Verification\n- [ ] Hot-to-neutral voltage: within spec (114V–126V for 120V nominal)\n- [ ] Hot-to-ground voltage: within spec\n- [ ] Neutral-to-ground voltage: <2V\n- [ ] Both outlets tested (top and bottom of duplex)\n\n### Step 4: GFCI Verification (if applicable)\n- [ ] TEST button trips the device\n- [ ] RESET button restores power\n- [ ] External GFCI tester confirms trip\n- [ ] Downstream outlets lose power when GFCI trips\n- [ ] Downstream outlets labeled \"GFCI Protected\"\n\n### Step 5: Load Test\n- [ ] Device powers a real load (lamp, tool, appliance) without flickering or heat\n- [ ] No buzzing, arcing sounds, or burning smell\n- [ ] After 5 minutes under load: touch cover plate — should not be warm\n- [ ] Plug fits snugly — not loose in receptacle slots\n\n### Step 6: Final Safety Verification\n- [ ] Breaker labeled correctly at panel\n- [ ] LOTO device removed from panel\n- [ ] All tools and materials removed from work area\n- [ ] **Zero deficiencies confirmed** ✓\n\n---\n\n## 11. Cleanup & Closeout\n\n### Cleanup\n1. Remove all debris, wire scraps, old receptacle, and packaging\n2. Clean wall area around cover plate (remove fingerprints, dust)\n3. Vacuum / sweep work area\n4. Dispose of old receptacle properly (e-waste if applicable)\n\n### Documentation\n1. Complete work order with:\n   - Actual time spent (start/end)\n   - Materials used (receptacle model, wire nuts, pigtails)\n   - Photos: before, during (wiring), after\n   - Any additional work performed or recommended\n   - Wire type identified (copper/aluminum)\n   - Test results (voltage readings, GFCI trip confirmation)\n2. Note any code concerns or recommended follow-up work:\n   - Other outlets on circuit needing upgrade\n   - Panel labeling corrections\n   - Aluminum wiring remediation recommendations\n3. Obtain customer sign-off on completed work\n\n### Customer Handoff\n- Demonstrate receptacle operation\n- Show GFCI test/reset procedure (if applicable)\n- Explain tamper-resistant feature (if customer is unfamiliar)\n- Identify circuit breaker location and label\n- Advise on monthly GFCI testing requirement\n- Provide manufacturer warranty information\n- Leave contact information for warranty service calls\n\n---\n\n## 12. Troubleshooting\n\n| Issue | Probable Cause | Resolution |\n|---|---|---|\n| Receptacle tester shows open ground | Ground wire not connected; broken ground path | Reconnect ground; check continuity back to panel |\n| Receptacle tester shows hot/neutral reverse | Hot and neutral wires swapped at terminals | Swap wires to correct terminals (black→brass, white→silver) |\n| Receptacle tester shows open neutral | Neutral wire not connected or broken | Check neutral connection; inspect for broken wire in box |\n| GFCI won't reset | No power to LINE terminals; defective device; LINE/LOAD reversed | Verify power at LINE terminals; check wiring; replace if defective |\n| GFCI trips immediately | Ground fault on LOAD side; neutral-ground bond downstream | Disconnect LOAD wires and reset; isolate fault on downstream circuit |\n| Outlet is warm to touch | Loose connection; wire gauge undersized; backstab connection failing | Re-terminate all connections with screw terminals; verify wire gauge |\n| Intermittent power | Loose terminal connection; backstab connection; damaged wire | Re-strip and re-terminate; convert backstab to screw terminal |\n| Buzzing or arcing sound | Loose connection; damaged receptacle; arcing at terminal | De-energize immediately; inspect and re-terminate or replace device |\n| Breaker trips when outlet is used | Short circuit; ground fault; overloaded circuit | Inspect wiring for shorts; check load calculation; isolate fault |\n| Plug falls out of receptacle | Worn contact springs in receptacle | Replace receptacle — contacts are not repairable |\n| Sparking when plugging in | Normal if brief; abnormal if sustained | Brief spark is normal (inrush current); sustained spark = loose connection or damaged device |\n\n---\n\n## 13. Quality Standards\n\n- All work shall comply with the National Electrical Code (NEC/NFPA 70) and local amendments\n- All materials shall be new, UL-listed, and rated for the intended use\n- All wire terminations shall be mechanically secure with no exposed copper outside the device\n- Screw terminal connections preferred over backstab (push-in) for reliability\n- GFCI protection shall be provided at all code-required locations\n- All replacement receptacles shall be tamper-resistant (TR) per NEC 406.12\n- Receptacle amperage shall match or be compatible with circuit breaker and wire gauge\n- Workmanship shall be neat, professional, and pass visual inspection\n- Customer property shall be protected and left clean\n- Circuit shall be properly labeled at the electrical panel\n\n---\n\n## 14. Revision History\n\n| Rev | Date | Author | Description |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| 1.0 | 2026-02-28 | — | Initial release |\n\n---\n\n*This SOP is a living document.     def search(self, query: str) -> list:
        """Search SOPs by keyword in sop_number, trade, task, description, tags."""

            f"sop_number.ilike.%{q}%,trade_name.ilike.%{q}%,task_name.ilike.%{q}%,description.ilike.%{q}%"
        ).execute()
        return results.data

    def list_all(self) -> list:
        """List all SOPs."""
        results = self.client.table("sops").select(
            "sop_number, trade_code, trade_name, task_name, revision, status, storage_path"
        ).order("sop_number").execute()
        return results.data

    def show(self, sop_number: str) -> dict:
        """Show full SOP details with cached data."""
        sop = self.client.table("sops").select("*").eq("sop_number", sop_number).execute()
        if not sop.data:
            return {"error": f"SOP {sop_number} not found"}
        sop_id = sop.data[0]["id"]
        checklists = self.client.table("sop_executive_checklists").select("*").eq("sop_id", sop_id).order("section_number,step_number").execute()
        steps = self.client.table("sop_detailed_steps").select("*").eq("sop_id", sop_id).order("section_number,step_number").execute()
        videos = self.client.table("sop_recommended_videos").select("*").eq("sop_id", sop_id).order("sort_order").execute()
        return {
            "sop": sop.data[0],
            "executive_checklists": checklists.data,
            "detailed_steps": steps.data,
            "recommended_videos": videos.data
        }

## Metadata
- **Name**: sop_library_manager
- **Version**: 1.0.0
- **Description**: Manages the SOP (Standard Operating Procedure) library in Supabase Storage and Database. Handles uploading, parsing, syncing, searching, and agent-driven creation of SOPs.
- **Tags**: sop, library, supabase, storage, database, procedures, trade, plumbing, electrical
- **Author**: Agent Zero
- **Created**: 2026-02-28

## Overview

This skill provides a complete lifecycle manager for Standard Operating Procedures stored as `.md` files in Supabase Storage (`apps/voicelog/sops/`). It parses structured SOP markdown into database tables for fast retrieval of executive checklists, detailed steps, and recommended videos.

### Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   SOP Library Flow                       │
│                                                         │
│  .md File ──► Upload to Storage ──► Parse Markdown      │
│                                         │               │
│                                         ▼               │
│                              ┌──────────────────┐       │
│                              │   sops (master)   │       │
│                              └────────┬─────────┘       │
│                    ┌──────────────────┼──────────────┐   │
│                    ▼                  ▼              ▼   │
│          sop_executive_    sop_detailed_   sop_recommended│
│          checklists        steps           _videos        │
│                                                         │
│  Search/Query ◄── Database Tables (fast retrieval)      │
│  Missing SOP? ──► Agent Request ──► Create + Upload     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Database Tables

| Table | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `sops` | Master index — SOP number, trade, task, revision, status, storage path, file hash |
| `sop_executive_checklists` | Cached executive checklist items per procedure phase |
| `sop_detailed_steps` | Cached detailed step content per procedure phase |
| `sop_recommended_videos` | Cached video links organized by procedure phase |
| `sop_sync_log` | Audit trail for all library operations |
| `v_sop_library` | Convenience view with content counts |

### Storage

- **Bucket**: `apps`
- **Path**: `voicelog/sops/{filename}.md`
- **Format**: Structured markdown following SOP Authoring Guide (SOP-META-001)

## Prerequisites

- Supabase local instance running (port 54321/54322)
- Migration `20260228000001_sop_library.sql` applied
- Python packages: `supabase` (auto-installed if missing)

## Procedures

### 1. Upload an SOP to the Library

Uploads a `.md` file to Supabase Storage, parses it, and syncs all structured data to the database.

```bash
cd /a0/usr/projects/timesheet_app/voicelog-scaffold
python skills/sop_library_manager/scripts/sop_manager.py upload <path-to-sop.md>
```

**Example:**
```bash
python skills/sop_library_manager/scripts/sop_manager.py upload docs/general/SOP_Plumbing_Sink_Replacement.md
```

**Output:** JSON with sop_id, sop_number, sync counts for checklists/steps/videos.

### 2. Sync Database from Storage

Re-parses SOP files from storage and updates the database. Detects changes via MD5 hash.

```bash
# Sync all SOPs
python skills/sop_library_manager/scripts/sop_manager.py sync --all

# Sync a specific SOP
python skills/sop_library_manager/scripts/sop_manager.py sync PLB-SOP-001
```

### 3. Search the Library

Search by keyword across SOP number, trade, task name, and description.

```bash
python skills/sop_library_manager/scripts/sop_manager.py search "plumbing"
python skills/sop_library_manager/scripts/sop_manager.py search "outlet"
python skills/sop_library_manager/scripts/sop_manager.py search "sink"
```

### 4. List All SOPs

```bash
python skills/sop_library_manager/scripts/sop_manager.py list
```

### 5. Show SOP Details with Cached Data

Retrieves the full SOP record plus all cached checklists, steps, and videos from the database.

```bash
python skills/sop_library_manager/scripts/sop_manager.py show PLB-SOP-001
```

### 6. Check if SOP Exists — Request Creation if Not

This is the key workflow for agent integration. Checks the library for a matching SOP. If none found, generates an agent request payload with the prompt to create one.

```bash
python skills/sop_library_manager/scripts/sop_manager.py check "Plumbing" "Water Heater Replacement"
```

**If found:** Returns matching SOP records.
**If not found:** Returns an `agent_request` payload:
```json
{
  "found": false,
  "message": "No SOP found for 'Plumbing — Water Heater Replacement'. Agent creation requested.",
  "agent_request": {
    "action": "create_sop",
    "sop_number": "PLB-SOP-002",
    "trade_code": "PLB",
    "trade_name": "Plumbing",
    "task_name": "Water Heater Replacement",
    "prompt": "Create a complete Standard Operating Procedure for Plumbing — Water Heater Replacement..."
  }
}
```

### 7. Agent-Driven SOP Creation Workflow

When `check` returns `found: false`, the agent should:

1. **Read the agent_request payload** from the check result
2. **Call a subordinate agent** (developer profile) with the prompt from the payload
3. **The subordinate creates the SOP** following the Authoring Guide
4. **Upload the new SOP** using the `upload` command
5. **Verify** using the `show` command

**Agent Zero integration example:**
```python
import json
import subprocess

# Step 1: Check if SOP exists
result = subprocess.run(
    ["python", "skills/sop_library_manager/scripts/sop_manager.py", "check", "HVAC", "Thermostat Replacement"],
    capture_output=True, text=True, cwd="/a0/usr/projects/timesheet_app/voicelog-scaffold"
)
check_result = json.loads(result.stdout)

if not check_result["found"]:
    # Step 2: Use the prompt to create the SOP via subordinate agent
    agent_prompt = check_result["agent_request"]["prompt"]
    # ... call_subordinate with developer profile and agent_prompt ...

    # Step 3: After creation, upload to library
    # python sop_manager.py upload <new_sop_file.md>
```

```
skills/sop_library_manager/
├── SKILL.md                          # This file
└── scripts/
    └── sop_manager.py                # Main management script (CLI)
```

## Environment Variables

| `SUPABASE_URL` | `http://127.0.0.1:54321` | Supabase API URL |
| `SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY` | Local dev key | Service role key for admin access |

## Troubleshooting

| Issue | Solution |
|---|---|
| `Connection refused` | Ensure Supabase is running: `supabase status` |
| `relation "sops" does not exist` | Apply migration: `supabase db reset` or run the SQL manually |
| `Bucket not found` | Script auto-creates the bucket; check storage permissions |
| `Parse returns 0 items` | Verify SOP follows the Authoring Guide format exactly |
| `Hash unchanged` | File content identical to last sync; force with `sync` command |

Handles uploading, parsing, syncing, searching, and agent-driven creation of SOPs.\n- **Tags**: sop, library, supabase, storage, database, procedures, trade, plumbing, electrical, hvac\n- **Author**: Agent Zero\n- **Created**: 2026-02-28\n- **Updated**: 2026-02-28\n\n## Overview\n\nThis skill provides a complete lifecycle manager for Standard Operating Procedures stored as `.md` files in Supabase Storage (`apps/voicelog/sops/`). It parses structured SOP markdown into database tables for fast retrieval of executive checklists, detailed steps, and recommended videos.\n\n### Architecture\n\n```\n┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐\n│                   SOP Library Flow                       │\n│                                                         │\n│  .md File ──► Upload to Storage ──► Parse Markdown      │\n│                                         │               │\n│                                         ▼               │\n│                              ┌──────────────────┐       │\n│                              │   sops (master)   │       │\n│                              └────────┬─────────┘       │\n│                    ┌──────────────────┼──────────────┐   │\n│                    ▼                  ▼              ▼   │\n│          sop_executive_    sop_detailed_   sop_recommended│\n│          checklists        steps           _videos        │\n│                                                         │\n│  Search/Query ◄── Database Tables (fast retrieval)      │\n│  Missing SOP? ──► Agent Request ──► Create + Upload     │\n└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n```\n\n### Database Tables\n\n| Table | Purpose |\n|---|---|\n| `sops` | Master index — SOP number, trade, task, revision, status, storage path, file hash |\n| `sop_executive_checklists` | Cached executive checklist items per procedure phase |\n| `sop_detailed_steps` | Cached detailed step content per procedure phase |\n| `sop_recommended_videos` | Cached video links organized by procedure phase |\n| `sop_sync_log` | Audit trail for all library operations |\n| `v_sop_library` | Convenience view with content counts |\n\n### Storage\n\n- **Bucket**: `apps`\n- **Path**: `voicelog/sops/{filename}.md`\n- **Format**: Structured markdown following SOP Authoring Guide (SOP-META-001)\n\n## Prerequisites\n\n- Supabase instance running (local or remote)\n- Migration `20260228000001_sop_library.sql` applied\n- Python packages: `supabase` (auto-installed if missing)\n\n## Procedures\n\n### 1. Upload an SOP to the Library\n\nUploads a `.md` file to Supabase Storage, parses it, and syncs all structured data to the database.\n\n```bash\ncd /a0/usr/projects/timesheet_app/voicelog-scaffold\npython skills/sop_library_manager/scripts/sop_manager.py upload <path-to-sop.md>\n```\n\n**Example:**\n```bash\npython skills/sop_library_manager/scripts/sop_manager.py upload docs/general/SOP_Plumbing_Sink_Replacement.md\n```\n\n**Output:** JSON with sop_id, sop_number, sync counts for checklists/steps/videos.\n\n### 2. Sync Database from Storage\n\nRe-parses SOP files from storage and updates the database. Detects changes via MD5 hash.\n\n```bash\n# Sync all SOPs\npython skills/sop_library_manager/scripts/sop_manager.py sync --all\n\n# Sync a specific SOP\npython skills/sop_library_manager/scripts/sop_manager.py sync PLB-SOP-001\n```\n\n### 3. Search the Library\n\nSearch by keyword across SOP number, trade, task name, and description.\n\n```bash\npython skills/sop_library_manager/scripts/sop_manager.py search \"plumbing\"\npython skills/sop_library_manager/scripts/sop_manager.py search \"outlet\"\npython skills/sop_library_manager/scripts/sop_manager.py search \"sink\"\n```\n\n### 4. List All SOPs\n\n```bash\npython skills/sop_library_manager/scripts/sop_manager.py list\n```\n\n### 5. Show SOP Details with Cached Data\n\nRetrieves the full SOP record plus all cached checklists, steps, and videos from the database.\n\n```bash\npython skills/sop_library_manager/scripts/sop_manager.py show PLB-SOP-001\n```\n\n### 6. Check if SOP Exists — Request Creation if Not\n\nThis is the key workflow for agent integration. Checks the library for a matching SOP. If none found, generates an agent request payload with the prompt to create one.\n\n```bash\npython skills/sop_library_manager/scripts/sop_manager.py check \"Plumbing\" \"Water Heater Replacement\"\n```\n\n**If found:** Returns matching SOP records.\n**If not found:** Returns an `agent_request` payload:\n```json\n{\n  \"found\": false,\n  \"message\": \"No SOP found for 'Plumbing — Water Heater Replacement'. Agent creation requested.\",\n  \"agent_request\": {\n    \"action\": \"create_sop\",\n    \"sop_number\": \"PLB-SOP-002\",\n    \"trade_code\": \"PLB\",\n    \"trade_name\": \"Plumbing\",\n    \"task_name\": \"Water Heater Replacement\",\n    \"prompt\": \"Create a complete Standard Operating Procedure for Plumbing — Water Heater Replacement...\"\n  }\n}\n```\n\n### 7. Agent-Driven SOP Creation Workflow\n\nWhen `check` returns `found: false`, the agent should:\n\n1. **Read the agent_request payload** from the check result\n2. **Call a subordinate agent** (developer profile) with the prompt from the payload\n3. **The subordinate creates the SOP** following the Authoring Guide\n4. **Upload the new SOP** using the `upload` command\n5. **Verify** using the `show` command\n\nSee **AGENT_GUIDE.md** for detailed step-by-step instructions with exact tool calls.\n\n## Documentation\n\n| Document | Purpose |\n|---|---|\n| `SKILL.md` | This file — skill manifest (agentskills.io standard) |\n| `README.md` | Human-readable overview with architecture, API reference, and schema docs |\n| `AGENT_GUIDE.md` | Step-by-step guide for Agent Zero agents with exact tool call examples |\n\n## File Tree\n\n```\nskills/sop_library_manager/\n├── SKILL.md                          # When running directly on the host, use `127.0.0.1`.\n\n## Troubleshooting\n\n| Issue | Solution |\n|---|---|\n| `Connection refused` | Ensure Supabase is running: `supabase status` |\n| `relation \"sops\" does not exist` | Apply migration: `supabase db reset` or run the SQL manually |\n| `Bucket not found` | Script auto-creates the bucket; check storage permissions |\n| `Parse returns 0 items` | Verify SOP follows the Authoring Guide format exactly |\n| `Hash unchanged` | File content identical to last sync; force with `sync` command |\n| `Connection refused` (Docker) | Set `SUPABASE_URL=http://host.docker.internal:54321` |\n\"\"\"\n\nwith open(os.path.join(base, 'SKILL.md'), 'w') as f:\n    f.write(skill_md)\nprint(f'✅ SKILL.md updated ({len(skill_md)} chars)')\n\n# Verify all three files exist\nfor fname in ['SKILL.md', 'README.md', 'AGENT_GUIDE.md']:\n    fpath = os.path.join(base, fname)\n    size = os.path.getsize(fpath)\n    print(f'  📄 {fname}: {size:,} bytes')\n"

    ],
    "headline": "Creating plumbing SOP for sink replacement",
    "tool_name": "code_execution_tool",
    "tool_args": {
        "runtime": "terminal",
        "session": 0,
        "reset": false,
        "code": "cat > /a0/usr/projects/timesheet_app/voicelog-scaffold/docs/general/SOP_Plumbing_Sink_Replacement.md << 'SOPEOF'
# Standard Operating Procedure
## Plumbing — Sink Replacement

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| **SOP Number** | PLB-SOP-001 |
| **Trade** | Plumbing |
| **Task** | Remove & Replace Sink (Kitchen / Bathroom / Utility) |
| **Revision** | 1.0 |
| **Effective Date** | 2026-02-26 |
| **Approved By** | _________________________ |
| **Estimated Duration** | 2 – 4 hours (varies by scope) |

---

## 1. Purpose

This procedure defines the safe, code-compliant steps for removing an existing sink and installing a replacement sink, including all associated supply lines, drain connections, and fixtures. It applies to kitchen, bathroom, and utility sinks in residential and light-commercial settings.

---

## 2. Scope

This SOP covers:
- Disconnection and removal of the existing sink, faucet, and drain assembly
- Preparation of the countertop or mounting surface
- Installation of the new sink, faucet, supply lines, and drain assembly
- Leak testing and final inspection

This SOP does **not** cover:
- Countertop fabrication or modification beyond standard sink cutout adjustments
- Relocation of supply or drain rough-in (see SOP PLB-SOP-010)
- Garbage disposal installation (see SOP PLB-SOP-005)
- Dishwasher connection (see SOP PLB-SOP-006)

---

## 3. References & Codes

- International Plumbing Code (IPC) — current edition
- Uniform Plumbing Code (UPC) — current edition (where applicable)
- Local jurisdiction amendments
- Manufacturer installation instructions for specific sink and faucet models
- OSHA 29 CFR 1926 — Construction Industry Standards

---

## 4. Personnel & Qualifications

| Role | Requirement |
|---|---|
| Lead Plumber | Licensed journeyman plumber or equivalent |
| Apprentice (if assisting) | Enrolled apprentice under direct supervision |
| Inspector (if required) | Local AHJ plumbing inspector for final sign-off |

---

## 5. Safety Requirements

### 5.1 Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)
- Safety glasses / goggles
- Work gloves (cut-resistant recommended)
- Knee pads (for under-cabinet work)
- Steel-toe boots
- Dust mask (if cutting or grinding)

### 5.2 Hazard Awareness
| Hazard | Mitigation |
|---|---|
| Water damage | Shut off supply valves; have towels/bucket ready |
| Sharp edges | Wear cut-resistant gloves; deburr all cut pipe |
| Mold / mildew exposure | Wear N95 mask if mold is present; report to supervisor |
| Electrical proximity | Verify no live wiring near work area; use GFCI-protected circuits |
| Back strain | Use proper lifting technique; get assistance for heavy sinks (cast iron, stone) |
| Lead solder (older homes) | Test if pre-1986 plumbing; follow lead-safe work practices if positive |

### 5.3 Pre-Work Safety Checklist
- [ ] Water supply shut off and verified (both hot and cold)
- [ ] Work area cleared and protected (drop cloths, floor protection)
- [ ] Electrical circuits near sink area identified
- [ ] Fire extinguisher accessible (if soldering required)
- [ ] First aid kit on site

---

## 6. Tools & Equipment

### 6.1 Required Tools
| Tool | Purpose |
|---|---|
| Adjustable wrench (2) | Supply line and fitting connections |
| Basin wrench | Faucet mounting nuts in tight spaces |
| Channel-lock pliers (10" & 12") | Slip-joint nuts, compression fittings |
| Pipe wrench (14") | Stubborn fittings, galvanized con

<< 2801 Characters hidden >>


2. Open the faucet to relieve pressure and drain residual water
3. If shut-off valves do not hold, shut off at the main and note valve replacement is required
4. Place bucket under connections

### Step 2: Disconnect Supply Lines
1. Disconnect hot and cold supply lines from shut-off valves using adjustable wrench
2. Disconnect supply lines from faucet using basin wrench
3. Allow lines to drain into bucket
4. Cap or plug open valve outlets if sink will be out for extended period

### Step 3: Disconnect Drain Assembly
1. Loosen slip-joint nut connecting tailpiece to P-trap (channel-lock pliers)
2. Loosen slip-joint nut connecting P-trap to wall stub-out or floor drain
3. Remove P-trap assembly — dump residual water into bucket
4. Stuff rag into open drain pipe to prevent sewer gas entry
5. If garbage disposal is present: disconnect electrical, disconnect dishwasher drain hose, remove disposal from sink flange

### Step 4: Disconnect Ancillary Connections
- Dishwasher drain hose (if connected to tailpiece or disposal)
- Filtered water dispenser lines
- Soap dispenser mounting
- Instant hot water dispenser

### Step 5: Remove Faucet (if not transferring to new sink)
1. Remove faucet mounting nuts from underside using basin wrench
2. Lift faucet assembly out from above
3. Clean mounting surface

### Step 6: Remove Sink

**For undermount sinks:**
1. Cut silicone sealant between sink rim and countertop using utility knife
2. Remove mounting clips from underside
3. Support sink from below while removing last clips
4. Lower sink carefully — **two-person lift for large/heavy sinks**

**For drop-in (top-mount) sinks:**
1. Cut caulk/sealant around sink rim using utility knife
2. Remove any mounting clips from underside
3. Lift sink straight up and out

**For wall-mount sinks:**
1. Disconnect all plumbing connections
2. Remove mounting bolts/screws from wall bracket
3. Lift sink off bracket — **support sink weight throughout**

**For pedestal sinks:**
1. Disconnect all plumbing connections
2. Remove sink from wall mounting
3. Remove pedestal base

### Step 7: Inspect & Prepare
1. Inspect countertop cutout for damage, rot, or mold
2. Inspect cabinet interior for water damage
3. Clean all old putty, caulk, and adhesive from countertop surface
4. Inspect and clean drain pipe — remove rag
5. **Document and photograph** any discovered damage
6. **Report issues to supervisor / customer** before proceeding

---

## 9. Procedure — Installation of New Sink

### Step 1: Dry-Fit the New Sink
1. Place new sink into cutout (drop-in) or hold in position (undermount) to verify fit
2. Check alignment with drain and supply rough-ins
3. Verify faucet holes align with selected faucet
4. Mark position if needed with painter's tape

### Step 2: Install Faucet on New Sink (Before Mounting)
> **Best practice:** Install faucet, strainer, and drain fittings on the sink before mounting — much easier access.

1. Insert faucet through mounting holes from above
2. Install rubber gasket and mounting hardware from below
3. Hand-tighten, then snug with basin wrench — **do not overtighten**
4. Connect supply line stubs to faucet inlets (hand-tight + 1/4 turn)

### Step 3: Install Basket Strainer / Drain Fitting

**Kitchen sink (basket strainer):**
1. Roll plumber's putty into 1/2" rope
2. Apply putty ring around strainer flange
3. Insert strainer into drain hole from above
4. From below: install rubber gasket → cardboard friction ring → locknut
5. Tighten locknut while holding strainer from above to prevent rotation
6. Trim excess putty from above with putty knife

**Bathroom sink (pop-up drain):**
1. Apply plumber's putty or silicone (per manufacturer) to drain flange
2. Insert drain body from above
3. Thread drain body into tailpiece from below
4. Install pop-up linkage and adjust

### Step 4: Mount the Sink

**Drop-in (top-mount):**
1. Apply continuous bead of silicone sealant around countertop cutout edge
2. Carefully lower sink into cutout — align with marks
3. Press down firmly and evenly to seat
4. Install mounting clips from below — tighten evenly in star pattern
5. Wipe excess sealant from above and below

**Undermount:**
1. Apply silicone sealant to sink rim or countertop underside per manufacturer
2. Lift sink into position from below — **two-person operation**
3. Install mounting clips/brackets — tighten evenly
4. Support sink with temporary bracing until sealant cures (if required)
5. Clean excess sealant

**Wall-mount:**
1. Install wall bracket at correct height (31" – 34" AFF standard, or ADA height 34" max)
2. Verify bracket is level and secured to blocking/studs
3. Hang sink on bracket
4. Secure with mounting bolts

### Step 5: Connect Drain Assembly
1. Install tailpiece to strainer/drain body — hand-tight slip joint
2. Assemble P-trap:
   - Trap arm into wall stub-out (ensure proper slope — 1/4" per foot minimum)
   - P-trap bend connecting tailpiece to trap arm
3. Tighten all slip-joint nuts — **hand-tight plus 1/4 turn with pliers**
4. Ensure trap weir is not below the crown weir of the trap arm
5. Verify horizontal distance from trap weir to vent does not exceed code maximums

> ⚠️ **Code Check:** P-trap must be accessible, must have water seal of 2" – 4", and must not be double-trapped.

### Step 6: Connect Supply Lines
1. Connect braided stainless supply lines to shut-off valves
   - Hand-tight + 1/4 turn with wrench
   - **Do not use Teflon tape on compression fittings**
2. If replacing shut-off valves:
   - Cut supply pipe cleanly with tubing cutter
   - Deburr inside and outside
   - Install new 1/4-turn ball valve (compression or push-fit)
3. Verify hot is on left, cold is on right (facing the sink)

### Step 7: Reconnect Ancillary Items
- Dishwasher drain hose to disposal or tailpiece air gap
- Filtered water line
- Soap dispenser
- Instant hot water dispenser
- Garbage disposal (if included in scope)

---

## 10. Testing & Commissioning

### Step 1: Visual Inspection
- [ ] All connections hand-checked for tightness
- [ ] P-trap properly assembled with correct slope
- [ ] Supply lines not kinked or stressed
- [ ] Sink is level and properly seated
- [ ] Caulk/sealant applied cleanly

### Step 2: Pressure Test — Supply Side
1. Slowly open cold shut-off valve
2. Check all cold-side connections for leaks (supply valve, supply line, faucet)
3. Slowly open hot shut-off valve
4. Check all hot-side connections for leaks
5. Run faucet for 2 minutes — check under sink with flashlight
6. Check faucet base and handles for leaks
7. **Wipe all connections dry, place paper towel underneath, wait 5 minutes, re-inspect**

### Step 3: Drain Test
1. Close sink stopper / plug drain
2. Fill sink to overflow level (or 75% capacity)
3. Release stopper — observe drain flow
4. Check all drain connections for leaks during full-flow drainage
5. Check P-trap connections
6. Check wall stub-out connection
7. Run water for 5 minutes — re-inspect all drain joints

### Step 4: Functional Test
- [ ] Hot and cold water flow correctly
- [ ] Faucet handles operate smoothly
- [ ] Sprayer functions (if equipped)
- [ ] Pop-up drain operates (bathroom)
- [ ] Basket strainer seats properly (kitchen)
- [ ] No vibration or water hammer
- [ ] Adequate flow rate (2.2 GPM kitchen / 1.5 GPM bathroom per federal standard)

### Step 5: Final Leak Check
- [ ] Dry all connections completely
- [ ] Place dry paper towels under all joints
- [ ] Run water intermittently over 15 minutes
- [ ] Inspect paper towels for any moisture
- [ ] **Zero leaks confirmed** ✓

---

## 11. Cleanup & Closeout

### 11.1 Site Cleanup
1. Remove all debris, old sink, packaging materials
2. Clean countertop and sink area
3. Clean cabinet interior — dry any residual moisture
4. Remove drop cloths and floor protection
5. Vacuum / sweep work area
6. Dispose of old materials per local regulations (check for lead solder on old pipes)

### 11.2 Documentation
1. Complete work order with:
   - Actual time spent (start/end)
   - Materials used (for billing and inventory)
   - Photos: before, during, after
   - Any additional work performed or recommended
2. Record shut-off valve condition and any replacements
3. Note any code concerns or recommended follow-up work
4. Obtain customer sign-off on completed work

### 11.3 Customer Handoff
- Demonstrate faucet operation
- Show location of shut-off valves
- Advise on caulk/sealant cure time (typically 24 hours — avoid heavy use)
- Provide manufacturer warranty information
- Leave contact information for warranty service calls

---

## 12. Troubleshooting

| Issue | Probable Cause | Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| Leak at supply connection | Over/under-tightened, cross-threaded | Disconnect, inspect threads, reconnect properly |
| Leak at drain slip joint | Washer misaligned, nut not tight | Disassemble, reseat washer, retighten |
| Slow drain | P-trap misaligned, improper slope | Verify 1/4"/ft slope, check for blockage |
| Faucet drips | Cartridge/valve seat issue | Check manufacturer troubleshooting; may be defective unit |
| Sink not level | Uneven mounting clips, countertop issue | Adjust clips evenly; shim if needed |
| Water hammer when faucet closes | High pressure, no arrestor | Install water hammer arrestor on supply lines |
| Sewer gas smell | Dry trap, improper venting | Verify trap seal; check vent connections |
| Sink pulls away from countertop | Insufficient sealant, missing clips | Remount with proper clips and sealant |

---

## 13. Quality Standards

- All work shall comply with the applicable plumbing code (IPC/UPC) and local amendments
- All materials shall be new, approved, and listed for the intended use
- All joints shall be watertight with zero leaks under operating pressure
- Drain connections shall maintain proper slope and trap seal
- Workmanship shall be neat, professional, and free of visible defects
- Customer property shall be protected and left clean

---

## 14. Revision History

| Rev | Date | Author | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.0 | 2026-02-26 | — | Initial release |

---

*This SOP is a living document. Report any errors, omissions, or improvement suggestions to the Operations Manager.*
SOPEOF

- All joints shall be watertight with zero leaks under operating pressure
- Drain connections shall maintain proper slope and trap seal
- Workmanship shall be neat, professional, and free of visible defects
- Customer property shall be protected and left clean

---

## 14. Revision History

| Rev | Date | Author | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.0 | 2026-02-26 | — | Initial release |

---

cat > /a0/usr/projects/timesheet_app/voicelog-scaffold/docs/general/SOP_Plumbing_Sink_Replacement.md << 'SOPEOF'
# Standard Operating Procedure
## Plumbing — Sink Replacement

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| **SOP Number** | PLB-SOP-001 |
| **Trade** | Plumbing |
| **Task** | Remove & Replace Sink (Kitchen / Bathroom / Utility) |
| **Revision** | 1.0 |
| **Effective Date** | 2026-02-26 |
| **Approved By** | _________________________ |
| **Estimated Duration** | 2 – 4 hours (varies by scope) |

---

## 1. Purpose

This procedure defines the safe, code-compliant steps for removing an existing sink and installing a replacement sink, including all associated supply lines, drain connections, and fixtures. It applies to kitchen, bathroom, and utility sinks in residential and light-commercial settings.

---

## 2. Scope

This SOP covers:
- Disconnection and removal of the existing sink, faucet, and drain assembly
- Preparation of the countertop or mounting surface
- Installation of the new sink, faucet, supply lines, and drain assembly
- Leak testing and final inspection

This SOP does **not** cover:
- Countertop fabrication or modification beyond standard sink cutout adjustments
- Relocation of supply or drain rough-in (see SOP PLB-SOP-010)
- Garbage disposal installation (see SOP PLB-SOP-005)
- Dishwasher connection (see SOP PLB-SOP-006)

---

## 3. References & Codes

- International Plumbing Code (IPC) — current edition
- Uniform Plumbing Code (UPC) — current edition (w

<< 11347 Characters hidden >>

lease stopper — observe drain flow
4. Check all drain connections for leaks during full-flow drainage
5. Check P-trap connections
6. Check wall stub-out connection
7. Run water for 5 minutes — re-inspect all drain joints

### Step 4: Functional Test
- [ ] Hot and cold water flow correctly
- [ ] Faucet handles operate smoothly
- [ ] Sprayer functions (if equipped)
- [ ] Pop-up drain operates (bathroom)
- [ ] Basket strainer seats properly (kitchen)
- [ ] No vibration or water hammer
- [ ] Adequate flow rate (2.2 GPM kitchen / 1.5 GPM bathroom per federal standard)

### Step 5: Final Leak Check
- [ ] Dry all connections completely
- [ ] Place dry paper towels under all joints
- [ ] Run water intermittently over 15 minutes
- [ ] Inspect paper towels for any moisture
- [ ] **Zero leaks confirmed** ✓

---

## 11. Cleanup & Closeout

### 11.1 Site Cleanup
1. Remove all debris, old sink, packaging materials
2. Clean countertop and sink area
3. Clean cabinet interior — dry any residual moisture
4. Remove drop cloths and floor protection
5. Vacuum / sweep work area
6. Dispose of old materials per local regulations (check for lead solder on old pipes)

### 11.2 Documentation
1. Complete work order with:
   - Actual time spent (start/end)
   - Materials used (for billing and inventory)
   - Photos: before, during, after
   - Any additional work performed or recommended
2. Record shut-off valve condition and any replacements
3. Note any code concerns or recommended follow-up work
4. Obtain customer sign-off on completed work

### 11.3 Customer Handoff
- Demonstrate faucet operation
- Show location of shut-off valves
- Advise on caulk/sealant cure time (typically 24 hours — avoid heavy use)
- Provide manufacturer warranty information
- Leave contact information for warranty service calls

---

## 12. Troubleshooting

| Issue | Probable Cause | Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| Leak at supply connection | Over/under-tightened, cross-threaded | Disconnect, inspect threads, reconnect properly |
| Leak at drain slip joint | Washer misaligned, nut not tight | Disassemble, reseat washer, retighten |
| Slow drain | P-trap misaligned, improper slope | Verify 1/4"/ft slope, check for blockage |
| Faucet drips | Cartridge/valve seat issue | Check manufacturer troubleshooting; may be defective unit |
| Sink not level | Uneven mounting clips, countertop issue | Adjust clips evenly; shim if needed |
| Water hammer when faucet closes | High pressure, no arrestor | Install water hammer arrestor on supply lines |
| Sewer gas smell | Dry trap, improper venting | Verify trap seal; check vent connections |
| Sink pulls away from countertop | Insufficient sealant, missing clips | Remount with proper clips and sealant |

---

## 13. Quality Standards

- All work shall comply with the applicable plumbing code (IPC/UPC) and local amendments
- All materials shall be new, approved, and listed for the intended use
- All joints shall be watertight with zero leaks under operating pressure
- Drain connections shall maintain proper slope and trap seal
- Workmanship shall be neat, professional, and free of visible defects
- Customer property shall be protected and left clean

---

## 14. Revision History

| Rev | Date | Author | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.0 | 2026-02-26 | — | Initial release |

---

*This SOP is a living document. Report any errors, omissions, or improvement suggestions to the Operations Manager.*
SOPEOF

kvps.solutions:
# Problem
 Create a professional Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for the plumbing trade specifically for sink replacement, suitable for inclusion in a work-order or timesheet platform.
# Solution
 1. Create a Markdown document (e.g., `SOP_Plumbing_Sink_Replacement.md`) with a structured layout including SOP Number, Trade, Task, and Revision details.
2. Define the **Purpose and Scope**, specifying types of sinks (kitchen, bathroom, utility) and what the procedure does not cover (e.g., countertop modification).
3. List **References and Codes** such as IPC (International Plumbing Code) and OSHA standards.
4. Detail **Safety Requirements**, including a PPE list (safety glasses, gloves, knee pads) and a hazard mitigation table (water damage, sharp edges, electrical proximity).
5. Provide a comprehensive **Tools and Materials** list, categorizing items like basin wrenches, channel-lock pliers, plumber's putty, and silicone sealant.
6. Outline a step-by-step **Removal Procedure**: shut off water, disconnect supply lines, disconnect drain assembly (P-trap), and remove the sink based on its type (undermount, drop-in, wall-mount).
7. Outline a step-by-step **Installation Procedure**: dry-fit, install faucet and strainer on the sink first, mount the sink with sealant/clips, and reconnect drain and supply lines.
8. Include a **Testing and Commissioning** section with specific checks: visual inspection, pressure test (supply side), drain test (filling sink to 75%), and a final leak check using the 'paper towel method'.
9. Add sections for **Cleanup, Documentation** (photos, customer sign-off), and a **Troubleshooting** table for common issues like slow drains or water hammer.
