new_s8 = '''## 8. Procedure — Removal of Existing Sink

### 📋 Removal Checklist (Quick Reference)

Use this checklist to track progress through the removal procedure. Each item corresponds to the detailed steps below.

| # | Task | ✓ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hot & cold shut-off valves closed | ☐ |
| 2 | Faucet opened to relieve pressure / drain residual water | ☐ |
| 3 | Bucket placed under connections | ☐ |
| 4 | Hot supply line disconnected | ☐ |
| 5 | Cold supply line disconnected | ☐ |
| 6 | Supply lines disconnected from faucet (basin wrench) | ☐ |
| 7 | Open valve outlets capped/plugged (if extended removal) | ☐ |
| 8 | P-trap slip-joint nut loosened (tailpiece side) | ☐ |
| 9 | P-trap slip-joint nut loosened (wall/floor side) | ☐ |
| 10 | P-trap removed and drained into bucket | ☐ |
| 11 | Rag stuffed into open drain pipe | ☐ |
| 12 | Garbage disposal disconnected (if present) | ☐ |
| 13 | Dishwasher drain hose disconnected (if present) | ☐ |
| 14 | Filtered water / soap dispenser / hot water dispenser disconnected | ☐ |
| 15 | Faucet mounting nuts removed (if not transferring) | ☐ |
| 16 | Faucet lifted out and mounting surface cleaned | ☐ |
| 17 | Sealant / caulk cut around sink perimeter | ☐ |
| 18 | Mounting clips removed | ☐ |
| 19 | Sink removed (two-person lift if heavy) | ☐ |
| 20 | Countertop cutout inspected for damage / rot / mold | ☐ |
| 21 | Cabinet interior inspected for water damage | ☐ |
| 22 | Old putty, caulk, adhesive cleaned from surface | ☐ |
| 23 | Drain pipe inspected and cleaned; rag removed | ☐ |
| 24 | Damage documented and photographed | ☐ |
| 25 | Issues reported to supervisor / customer (if any) | ☐ |

---

### Step 1: Shut Off Water Supply
1. Close both hot and cold shut-off valves under the sink
2. Open the faucet to relieve pressure and drain residual water
3. If shut-off valves do not hold, shut off at the main and note valve replacement is required
4. Place bucket under connections

### Step 2: Disconnect Supply Lines
1. Disconnect hot and cold supply lines from shut-off valves using adjustable wrench
2. Disconnect supply lines from faucet using basin wrench
3. Allow lines to drain into bucket
4. Cap or plug open valve outlets if sink will be out for extended period

### Step 3: Disconnect Drain Assembly
1. Loosen slip-joint nut connecting tailpiece to P-trap (channel-lock pliers)
2. Loosen slip-joint nut connecting P-trap to wall stub-out or floor drain
3. Remove P-trap assembly — dump residual water into bucket
4. Stuff rag into open drain pipe to prevent sewer gas entry
5. If garbage disposal is present: disconnect electrical, disconnect dishwasher drain hose, remove disposal from sink flange

### Step 4: Disconnect Ancillary Connections
- Dishwasher drain hose (if connected to tailpiece or disposal)
- Filtered water dispenser lines
- Soap dispenser mounting
- Instant hot water dispenser

### Step 5: Remove Faucet (if not transferring to new sink)
1. Remove faucet mounting nuts from underside using basin wrench
2. Lift faucet assembly out from above
3. Clean mounting surface

### Step 6: Remove Sink

**For undermount sinks:**
1. Cut silicone sealant between sink rim and countertop using utility knife
2. Remove mounting clips from underside
3. Support sink from below while removing last clips
4. Lower sink carefully — **two-person lift for large/heavy sinks**

**For drop-in (top-mount) sinks:**
1. Cut caulk/sealant around sink rim using utility knife
2. Remove any mounting clips from underside
3. Lift sink straight up and out

**For wall-mount sinks:**
1. Disconnect all plumbing connections
2. Remove mounting bolts/screws from wall bracket
3. Lift sink off bracket — **support sink weight throughout**

**For pedestal sinks:**
1. Disconnect all plumbing connections
2. Remove sink from wall mounting
3. Remove pedestal base

### Step 7: Inspect & Prepare
1. Inspect countertop cutout for damage, rot, or mold
2. Inspect cabinet interior for water damage
3. Clean all old putty, caulk, and adhesive from countertop surface
4. Inspect and clean drain pipe — remove rag
5. **Document and photograph** any discovered damage
6. **Report issues to supervisor / customer** before proceeding

---

'''

new_s9 = '''## 9. Procedure — Installation of New Sink

### 📋 Installation Checklist (Quick Reference)

Use this checklist to track progress through the installation procedure. Each item corresponds to the detailed steps below.

| # | Task | ✓ |
|---|---|---|
| **Dry-Fit & Prep** | | |
| 1 | New sink placed in cutout / held in position — fit verified | ☐ |
| 2 | Alignment with drain and supply rough-ins confirmed | ☐ |
| 3 | Faucet holes align with selected faucet | ☐ |
| 4 | Position marked with painter\'s tape (if needed) | ☐ |
| **Faucet Installation (on bench)** | | |
| 5 | Faucet inserted through mounting holes | ☐ |
| 6 | Rubber gasket and mounting hardware installed from below | ☐ |
| 7 | Faucet tightened with basin wrench (not overtightened) | ☐ |
| 8 | Supply line stubs connected to faucet inlets | ☐ |
| **Drain Fitting Installation** | | |
| 9 | Plumber\'s putty / silicone applied to strainer/drain flange | ☐ |
| 10 | Strainer / drain body inserted from above | ☐ |
| 11 | Gasket → friction ring → locknut installed from below | ☐ |
| 12 | Locknut tightened; excess putty trimmed | ☐ |
| 13 | Pop-up linkage installed and adjusted (bathroom only) | ☐ |
| **Sink Mounting** | | |
| 14 | Silicone sealant applied to cutout edge / sink rim | ☐ |
| 15 | Sink set into position (two-person for undermount/heavy) | ☐ |
| 16 | Mounting clips installed and tightened evenly (star pattern) | ☐ |
| 17 | Excess sealant wiped clean (above and below) | ☐ |
| 18 | Sink is level — verified with torpedo level | ☐ |
| **Drain Assembly** | | |
| 19 | Tailpiece installed to strainer/drain body | ☐ |
| 20 | Trap arm inserted into wall stub-out (slope ≥ 1/4"/ft) | ☐ |
| 21 | P-trap bend connecting tailpiece to trap arm | ☐ |
| 22 | All slip-joint nuts tightened (hand-tight + 1/4 turn) | ☐ |
| 23 | Trap weir height and vent distance verified per code | ☐ |
| **Supply Line Connection** | | |
| 24 | Cold supply line connected to shut-off valve | ☐ |
| 25 | Hot supply line connected to shut-off valve | ☐ |
| 26 | Shut-off valves replaced if needed (1/4-turn ball valves) | ☐ |
| 27 | Hot = left, Cold = right verified | ☐ |
| 28 | No kinks or stress on supply lines | ☐ |
| **Ancillary Reconnections** | | |
| 29 | Dishwasher drain hose reconnected (if applicable) | ☐ |
| 30 | Filtered water line reconnected (if applicable) | ☐ |
| 31 | Soap dispenser reinstalled (if applicable) | ☐ |
| 32 | Instant hot water dispenser reconnected (if applicable) | ☐ |
| 33 | Garbage disposal installed (if in scope) | ☐ |
| **Ready for Testing** | | |
| 34 | All connections visually inspected and hand-checked | ☐ |
| 35 | Work area clean and tools clear of cabinet | ☐ |
| 36 | Proceed to Section 10 — Testing & Commissioning | ☐ |

---

### Step 1: Dry-Fit the New Sink
1. Place new sink into cutout (drop-in) or hold in position (undermount) to verify fit
2. Check alignment with drain and supply rough-ins
3. Verify faucet holes align with selected faucet
4. Mark position if needed with painter\'s tape

### Step 2: Install Faucet on New Sink (Before Mounting)
> **Best practice:** Install faucet, strainer, and drain fittings on the sink before mounting — much easier access.

1. Insert faucet through mounting holes from above
2. Install rubber gasket and mounting hardware from below
3. Hand-tighten, then snug with basin wrench — **do not overtighten**
4. Connect supply line stubs to faucet inlets (hand-tight + 1/4 turn)

### Step 3: Install Basket Strainer / Drain Fitting

**Kitchen sink (basket strainer):**
1. Roll plumber\'s putty into 1/2" rope
2. Apply putty ring around strainer flange
3. Insert strainer into drain hole from above
4. From below: install rubber gasket → cardboard friction ring → locknut
5. Tighten locknut while holding strainer from above to prevent rotation
6. Trim excess putty from above with putty knife

**Bathroom sink (pop-up drain):**
1. Apply plumber\'s putty or silicone (per manufacturer) to drain flange
2. Insert drain body from above
3. Thread drain body into tailpiece from below
4. Install pop-up linkage and adjust

### Step 4: Mount the Sink

**Drop-in (top-mount):**
1. Apply continuous bead of silicone sealant around countertop cutout edge
2. Carefully lower sink into cutout — align with marks
3. Press down firmly and evenly to seat
4. Install mounting clips from below — tighten evenly in star pattern
5. Wipe excess sealant from above and below

**Undermount:**
1. Apply silicone sealant to sink rim or countertop underside per manufacturer
2. Lift sink into position from below — **two-person operation**
3. Install mounting clips/brackets — tighten evenly
4. Support sink with temporary bracing until sealant cures (if required)
5. Clean excess sealant

**Wall-mount:**
1. Install wall bracket at correct height (31" – 34" AFF standard, or ADA height 34" max)
2. Verify bracket is level and secured to blocking/studs
3. Hang sink on bracket
4. Secure with mounting bolts

### Step 5: Connect Drain Assembly
1. Install tailpiece to strainer/drain body — hand-tight slip joint
2. Assemble P-trap:
   - Trap arm into wall stub-out (ensure proper slope — 1/4" per foot minimum)
   - P-trap bend connecting tailpiece to trap arm
3. Tighten all slip-joint nuts — **hand-tight plus 1/4 turn with pliers**
4. Ensure trap weir is not below the crown weir of the trap arm
5. Verify horizontal distance from trap weir to vent does not exceed code maximums

> ⚠️ **Code Check:** P-trap must be accessible, must have water seal of 2" – 4", and must not be double-trapped.

### Step 6: Connect Supply Lines
1. Connect braided stainless supply lines to shut-off valves
   - Hand-tight + 1/4 turn with wrench
   - **Do not use Teflon tape on compression fittings**
2. If replacing shut-off valves:
   - Cut supply pipe cleanly with tubing cutter
   - Deburr inside and outside
   - Install new 1/4-turn ball valve (compression or push-fit)
3. Verify hot is on left, cold is on right (facing the sink)

### Step 7: Reconnect Ancillary Items
- Dishwasher drain hose to disposal or tailpiece air gap
- Filtered water line
- Soap dispenser
- Instant hot water dispenser
- Garbage disposal (if included in scope)

---

'''

Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for plumbing sink replacement (PLB-SOP-001), located at /a0/usr/projects/timesheet_app/voicelog-scaffold/docs/general/SOP_Plumbing_Sink_Replacement.md. This IPC/UPC compliant document covers kitchen, bathroom, and utility sinks. kvps.solutions:
# Problem
Create a comprehensive, professional Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for plumbing sink replacement, optimized for digital work-order platforms and field use.

# Solution
1. **SOP Structure**: Use a Markdown format (`SOP_Plumbing_Sink_Replacement.md`) including SOP Number, Trade, Task, Revision details, Purpose, Scope (kitchen, bathroom, utility), and References (IPC/OSHA).
2. **Safety & Qualifications**: Detail Personnel Qualifications and Safety Requirements, including PPE (glasses, gloves, knee pads) and a hazard mitigation table (water damage, sharp edges, electrical proximity).
3. **Tools & Materials**: Categorize essential items such as basin wrenches, channel-lock pliers, plumber's putty, and silicone sealant.
4. **Removal Procedure (Section 8)**: Step-by-step guide for water shut-off, supply/drain disconnection, and specific removal techniques for undermount, drop-in, wall-mount, and pedestal sinks.
5. **Installation Procedure (Section 9)**: Detailed workflow covering dry-fitting, pre-mounting faucets/strainers, sink mounting with sealant/clips, and final plumbing reconnections.
6. **Testing & Commissioning (Section 10)**: Implement specific tests: Visual inspection, Pressure test (supply side), Drain test (full-flow/75% capacity), and Functional test using the 'paper towel method' for leak detection.
7. **Quality Control**: Integrate 'Quick Reference' checklists using Markdown table format (☐) for removal and installation phases to ensure workflow consistency.
8. **Finalization**: Include sections for Cleanup, Documentation (photos, customer sign-off), and a Troubleshooting table for common issues like slow drains or water hammer.

kvps.memories:
Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for plumbing sink replacement (PLB-SOP-001), located at /a0/usr/projects/timesheet_app/voicelog-scaffold/docs/general/SOP_Plumbing_Sink_Replacement.md. This IPC/UPC compliant document covers kitchen, bathroom, and utility sinks. It features a two-tier checklist system: 

1. Executive Checklist (Priority Groups):
- Removal: 1. Isolate Services, 2. Disconnect All Plumbing, 3. Remove Fixtures, 4. Inspect & Prepare Surface, 5. Document & Report.
- Installation: 1. Dry-Fit & Pre-Mount Assembly, 2. Mount & Seal Sink, 3. Connect Drain System, 4. Connect Water Supply, 5. Reconnect Ancillaries & Prep for Test.

2. Detailed Checklist: Contains 25 items for removal and 36 items for installation.

The SOP covers safety (PPE, hazards), required tools/materials, pre-installation assessment, removal/installation procedures, testing/commissioning, and troubleshooting.
kvps.solutions:
# Problem
Create a comprehensive, professional Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for plumbing sink replacement, optimized for digital work-order platforms and field use, featuring a multi-tier checklist system.

# Solution
1. **SOP Structure**: Use Markdown format including SOP Number, Trade, Task, Revision details, Purpose, Scope (kitchen, bathroom, utility), and References (IPC/OSHA).
2. **Safety & Qualifications**: Detail Personnel Qualifications and Safety Requirements, including PPE (glasses, gloves, knee pads) and a hazard mitigation table (water damage, sharp edges, electrical proximity).
3. **Tools & Materials**: Categorize essential items such as basin wrenches, channel-lock pliers, plumber's putty, and silicone sealant.
4. **Removal Procedure (Section 8)**: Step-by-step guide for water isolation, supply/drain disconnection, and specific removal techniques for undermount, drop-in, wall-mount, and pedestal sinks.
5. **Installation Procedure (Section 9)**: Detailed workflow covering dry-fitting, pre-mounting faucets/strainers (bench assembly), sink mounting with sealant/clips, and final plumbing reconnections.
6. **Two-Tier Checklist System**: 
    - **Executive Checklist**: Group tasks into exactly 5 priority-ranked groups (Isolate Services, Disconnect Plumbing, Remove Fixtures, Inspect/Prepare, Document/Report) for high-level tracking.
    - **Detailed Checklist**: Granular sub-tasks (e.g., 'Rag stuffed into drain', 'Hot supply line disconnected') mapped to executive groups.
7. **Testing & Commissioning (Section 10)**: Implement specific tests: Visual inspection, Pressure test (supply side), Drain test (fill/release at 75% capacity), and Functional test using the 'paper towel method' for leak detection.
8. **Finalization & Quality Control**: Include sections for Cleanup, Documentation (photos, customer sign-off), and a Troubleshooting table for common issues like slow drains or water hammer.

kvps.solutions:
# Problem
Create a comprehensive, professional Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for plumbing sink replacement, optimized for digital work-order platforms and field use, featuring a multi-tier checklist system.

# Solution
1. **SOP Structure & Documentation**: Use Markdown format (`.md`) including SOP Number, Trade, Task, Revision details, Purpose, Scope (kitchen, bathroom, utility), and References (IPC/UPC/OSHA). Use clear headers, tables for tool/material lists, and callout blocks for safety warnings.
2. **Safety & Qualifications**: Detail Personnel Qualifications and Safety Requirements, including PPE (glasses, gloves, knee pads) and a hazard mitigation table (water damage, sharp edges, electrical proximity).
3. **Tools & Materials**: Categorize essential items such as basin wrenches, channel-lock pliers, plumber's putty, and silicone sealant.
4. **Removal Procedure**: Step-by-step guide for water isolation, supply/drain disconnection, and specific removal techniques for undermount, drop-in, wall-mount, and pedestal sinks. Ensure surface preparation is included after removal.
5. **Installation Procedure**: Detailed workflow covering dry-fitting, 'on-bench' assembly (installing faucet/strainer before mounting), sink mounting with sealant/clips, and final plumbing reconnections.
6. **Multi-Tier Checklist System**:
    - **Executive Checklist**: Create a high-level table with columns for 'Priority', 'Group', 'Tasks', and '✓'. Group tasks into exactly 5 priority-ranked phases: 1. Isolate Services, 2. Disconnect/Remove, 3. Prepare/Inspect, 4. Install/Reconnect, 5. Document/Report.
    - **Detailed Checklist**: Granular item-by-item sub-tasks (e.g., 'Rag stuffed into drain', 'Hot supply line disconnected') mapped to executive groups for field worker tracking.
7. **Testing & Commissioning**: Implement specific tests: Visual inspection, Pressure test (supply side), Drain test (fill/release at 75% capacity), and Functional test using the 'paper towel method' for leak detection.
8. **Finalization & Quality Control**: Include sections for Cleanup, Documentation (photos, customer sign-off), and a Troubleshooting table for common issues like slow drains, water hammer, or leaks.

python> filepath = '/a0/usr/projects/timesheet_app/voicelog-scaffold/docs/general/SOP_Plumbing_Sink_Replacem...

## 8. Procedure — Removal of Existing Sink

### 📋 Removal Phase Groups (Executive Checklist)

Complete each group in order. Check off the group when **all tasks within it are finished**.

| Priority | Group | Tasks Covered | ✓ |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| **1** | **Isolate Services** — Shut off water supply, relieve pressure, position bucket | Steps 1 (items 1–3) | ☐ |
| **2** | **Disconnect All Plumbing** — Detach supply lines, drain assembly, disposal, and ancillary connections | Steps 2–4 (items 4–14) | ☐ |
| **3** | **Remove Fixtures** — Remove faucet and sink from countertop/wall | Steps 5–6 (items 15–19) | ☐ |
| **4** | **Inspect & Prepare Surface** — Check for damage, rot, mold; clean all surfaces; clear drain pipe | Step 7 (items 20–23) | ☐ |
| **5** | **Document & Report** — Photograph conditions, report issues to supervisor/customer, get authorization | Step 7 cont. (items 24–25) | ☐ |

> ✅ **All 5 groups complete?** → Proceed to Section 9 — Installation.

---

### 📋 Removal Checklist (Quick Reference)

Use this checklist to track progress through the removal procedure. Each item corresponds to the detailed steps below.

| # | Task | ✓ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hot & cold shut-off valves closed | ☐ |
| 2 | Faucet opened to relieve pressure / drain residual water | ☐ |
| 3 | Bucket placed under connections | ☐ |
| 4 | Hot supply line disconnected | ☐ |
| 5 | Cold supply line disconnected | ☐ |
| 6 | Supply lines disconnected from faucet (basin wrench) | ☐ |
| 7 | Open valve outlets capped/plugged (if extended removal) | ☐ |
| 8 | P-trap slip-joint nut loosened (tailpiece side) | ☐ |
| 9 | P-trap slip-joint nut loosened (wall/floor side) | ☐ |
| 10 | P-trap removed and drained into bucket | ☐ |
| 11 | Rag stuffed into open drain pipe | ☐ |
| 12 | Garbage disposal disconnected (if present) | ☐ |
| 13 | Dishwasher drain hose disconnected (if present) | ☐ |
| 14 | Filtered water / soap dispenser / hot water dispenser disconnected | ☐ |
| 15 | Faucet mounting nuts removed (if not transferring) | ☐ |
| 16 | Faucet lifted out and mounting surface cleaned | ☐ |
| 17 | Sealant / caulk cut around sink perimeter | ☐ |
| 18 | Mounting clips removed | ☐ |
| 19 | Sink removed (two-person lift if heavy) | ☐ |
| 20 | Countertop cutout inspected for damage / rot / mold | ☐ |
| 21 | Cabinet interior inspected for water damage | ☐ |
| 22 | Old putty, caulk, adhesive cleaned from surface | ☐ |
| 23 | Drain pipe inspected and cleaned; rag removed | ☐ |
| 24 | Damage documented and photographed | ☐ |
| 25 | Issues reported to supervisor / customer (if any) | ☐ |

---

### Step 1: Shut Off Water Supply
1. Close both hot and cold shut-off valves under the sink
2. Open the faucet to relieve pressure and drain residual water
3. If shut-off valves do not hold, shut off at the main and note valve replacement is required
4. Place bucket under connections

### Step 2: Disconnect Supply Lines
1. Disconnect hot and cold supply lines from shut-off valves using adjustable wrench
2. Disconnect supply lines from faucet using basin wrench
3. Allow lines to drain into bucket
4. Cap or plug open valve outlets if sink will be out for extended period

### Step 3: Disconnect Drain Assembly
1. Loosen slip-joint nut connecting tailpiece to P-trap (channel-lock pliers)
2. Loosen slip-joint nut connecting P-trap to wall stub-out or floor drain
3. Remove P-trap assembly — dump residual water into bucket
4. Stuff rag into open drain pipe to prevent sewer gas entry
5. If garbage disposal is present: disconnect electrical, disconnect dishwasher drain hose, remove disposal from sink flange

### Step 4: Disconnect Ancillary Connections
- Dishwasher drain hose (if connected to tailpiece or disposal)
- Filtered water dispenser lines
- Soap dispenser mounting
- Instant hot water dispenser

### Step 5: Remove Faucet (if not transferring to new sink)
1. Remove faucet mounting nuts from underside using basin wrench
2. Lift faucet assembly out from above
3. Clean mounting surface

### Step 6: Remove Sink

**For undermount sinks:**
1. Cut silicone sealant between sink rim and countertop using utility knife
2. Remove mounting clips from underside
3. Support sink from below while removing last clips
4. Lower sink carefully — **two-person lift for large/heavy sinks**

**For drop-in (top-mount) sinks:**
1. Cut caulk/sealant around sink rim using utility knife
2. Remove any mounting clips from underside
3. Lift sink straight up and out

**For wall-mount sinks:**
1. Disconnect all plumbing connections
2. Remove mounting bolts/screws from wall bracket
3. Lift sink off bracket — **support sink weight throughout**

**For pedestal sinks:**
1. Disconnect all plumbing connections
2. Remove sink from wall mounting
3. Remove pedestal base

### Step 7: Inspect & Prepare
1. Inspect countertop cutout for damage, rot, or mold
2. Inspect cabinet interior for water damage
3. Clean all old putty, caulk, and adhesive from countertop surface
4. Inspect and clean drain pipe — remove rag
5. **Document and photograph** any discovered damage
6. **Report issues to supervisor / customer** before proceeding

---

## 9. Procedure — Installation of New Sink

### 📋 Installation Phase Groups (Executive Checklist)

Complete each group in order. Check off the group when **all tasks within it are finished**.

| Priority | Group | Tasks | ✓ |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| **1** | **Dry-Fit & Pre-Mount Assembly** | Verify sink fit, install faucet and drain fittings on bench before mounting | ☐ |
| **2** | **Mount & Seal Sink** | Set sink into position, install clips, apply sealant, verify level | ☐ |
| **3** | **Connect Drain System** | Install tailpiece, P-trap, trap arm; verify slope and code compliance | ☐ |
| **4** | **Connect Water Supply** | Attach supply lines to shut-off valves, replace valves if needed, verify hot/cold orientation | ☐ |
| **5** | **Reconnect Ancillaries & Prep for Test** | Reattach dishwasher, disposal, dispensers; final visual check | ☐ |

> ✅ **All 5 groups complete?** → Proceed to Section 10 — Testing & Commissioning.

---

### Step 1: Dry-Fit the New Sink
1. Place new sink into cutout (drop-in) or hold in position (undermount) to verify fit
2. Check alignment with drain and supply rough-ins
3. Verify faucet holes align with selected faucet
4. Mark position if needed with painter's tape

### Step 2: Install Faucet on New Sink (Before Mounting)
> **Best practice:** Install faucet, strainer, and drain fittings on the sink before mounting — much easier access.

1. Insert faucet through mounting holes from above
2. Install rubber gasket and mounting hardware from below
3. Hand-tighten, then snug with basin wrench — **do not overtighten**
4. Connect supply line stubs to faucet inlets (hand-tight + 1/4 turn)

### Step 3: Install Basket Strainer / Drain Fitting

**Kitchen sink (basket strainer):**
1. Roll plumber's putty into 1/2" rope
2. Apply putty ring around strainer flange
3. Insert strainer into drain hole from above
4. From below: install rubber gasket → cardboard friction ring → locknut
5. Tighten locknut while holding strainer from above to prevent rotation
6. Trim excess putty from above with putty knife

**Bathroom sink (pop-up drain):**
1. Apply plumber's putty or silicone (per manufacturer) to drain flange
2. Insert drain body from above
3. Thread drain body into tailpiece from below
4. Install pop-up linkage and adjust

### Step 4: Mount the Sink

**Drop-in (top-mount):**
1. Apply continuous bead of silicone sealant around countertop cutout edge
2. Carefully lower sink into cutout — align with marks
3. Press down firmly and evenly to seat
4. Install mounting clips from below — tighten evenly in star pattern
5. Wipe excess sealant from above and below

**Undermount:**
1. Apply silicone sealant to sink rim or countertop underside per manufacturer
2. Lift sink into position from below — **two-person operation**
3. Install mounting clips/brackets — tighten evenly
4. Support sink with temporary bracing until sealant cures (if required)
5. Clean excess sealant

**Wall-mount:**
1. Install wall bracket at correct height (31" – 34" AFF standard, or ADA height 34" max)
2. Verify bracket is level and secured to blocking/studs
3. Hang sink on bracket
4. Secure with mounting bolts

### Step 5: Connect Drain Assembly
1. Install tailpiece to strainer/drain body — hand-tight slip joint
2. Assemble P-trap:
   - Trap arm into wall stub-out (ensure proper slope — 1/4" per foot minimum)
   - P-trap bend connecting tailpiece to trap arm
3. Tighten all slip-joint nuts — **hand-tight plus 1/4 turn with pliers**
4. Ensure trap weir is not below the crown weir of the trap arm
5. Verify horizontal distance from trap weir to vent does not exceed code maximums

> ⚠️ **Code Check:** P-trap must be accessible, must have water seal of 2" – 4", and must not be double-trapped.

### Step 6: Connect Supply Lines
1. Connect braided stainless supply lines to shut-off valves
   - Hand-tight + 1/4 turn with wrench
   - **Do not use Teflon tape on compression fittings**
2. If replacing shut-off valves:
   - Cut supply pipe cleanly with tubing cutter
   - Deburr inside and outside
   - Install new 1/4-turn ball valve (compression or push-fit)
3. Verify hot is on left, cold is on right (facing the sink)

### Step 7: Reconnect Ancillary Items
- Dishwasher drain hose to disposal or tailpiece air gap
- Filtered water line
- Soap dispenser
- Instant hot water dispenser
- Garbage disposal (if included in scope)

---

new_s8 = r'''## 8. Procedure — Removal of Existing Sink

### Executive Checklist

Complete each step in order. Check off the step when **all tasks within it are finished**.

| Step | Step Name | Tasks | ✓ |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| **1** | **Isolate Services** | Shut off water supply, relieve pressure, position bucket | ☐ |
| **2** | **Disconnect All Plumbing** | Detach supply lines, drain assembly, disposal, and ancillary connections | ☐ |
| **3** | **Remove Fixtures** | Remove faucet and sink from countertop/wall | ☐ |
| **4** | **Inspect & Prepare Surface** | Check for damage, rot, mold; clean all surfaces; clear drain pipe | ☐ |
| **5** | **Document & Report** | Photograph conditions, report issues to supervisor/customer, get authorization | ☐ |

> ✅ **All 5 steps complete?** → Proceed to Section 9 — Installation.

---

### Detailed Steps

#### Step 1: Isolate Services
1. Close both hot and cold shut-off valves under the sink
2. Open the faucet to relieve pressure and drain residual water
3. If shut-off valves do not hold, shut off at the main and note valve replacement is required
4. Place bucket under connections

#### Step 2: Disconnect All Plumbing

**Supply lines:**
1. Disconnect hot and cold supply lines from shut-off valves using adjustable wrench
2. Disconnect supply lines from faucet using basin wrench
3. Allow lines to drain into bucket
4. Cap or plug open valve outlets if sink will be out for extended period

**Drain assembly:**
1. Loosen slip-joint nut connecting tailpiece to P-trap (channel-lock pliers)
2. Loosen slip-joint nut connecting P-trap to wall stub-out or floor drain
3. Remove P-trap assembly — dump residual water into bucket
4. Stuff rag into open drain pipe to prevent sewer gas entry
5. If garbage disposal is present: disconnect electrical, disconnect dishwasher drain hose, remove disposal from sink flange

**Ancillary connections:**
- Dishwasher drain hose (if connected to tailpiece or disposal)
- Filtered water dispenser lines
- Soap dispenser mounting
- Instant hot water dispenser

#### Step 3: Remove Fixtures

**Faucet (if not transferring to new sink):**
1. Remove faucet mounting nuts from underside using basin wrench
2. Lift faucet assembly out from above
3. Clean mounting surface

**Sink removal — undermount sinks:**
1. Cut silicone sealant between sink rim and countertop using utility knife
2. Remove mounting clips from underside
3. Support sink from below while removing last clips
4. Lower sink carefully — **two-person lift for large/heavy sinks**

**Sink removal — drop-in (top-mount) sinks:**
1. Cut caulk/sealant around sink rim using utility knife
2. Remove any mounting clips from underside
3. Lift sink straight up and out

**Sink removal — wall-mount sinks:**
1. Disconnect all plumbing connections
2. Remove mounting bolts/screws from wall bracket
3. Lift sink off bracket — **support sink weight throughout**

**Sink removal — pedestal sinks:**
1. Disconnect all plumbing connections
2. Remove sink from wall mounting
3. Remove pedestal base

#### Step 4: Inspect & Prepare Surface
1. Inspect countertop cutout for damage, rot, or mold
2. Inspect cabinet interior for water damage
3. Clean all old putty, caulk, and adhesive from countertop surface
4. Inspect and clean drain pipe — remove rag

#### Step 5: Document & Report
1. **Document and photograph** any discovered damage
2. **Report issues to supervisor / customer** before proceeding
3. Obtain authorization for any additional work required

---

'''

new_s9 = r'''## 9. Procedure — Installation of New Sink

### Executive Checklist

Complete each step in order. Check off the step when **all tasks within it are finished**.

| Step | Step Name | Tasks | ✓ |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| **1** | **Dry-Fit & Pre-Mount Assembly** | Verify sink fit, install faucet and drain fittings on bench before mounting | ☐ |
| **2** | **Mount & Seal Sink** | Set sink into position, install clips, apply sealant, verify level | ☐ |
| **3** | **Connect Drain System** | Install tailpiece, P-trap, trap arm; verify slope and code compliance | ☐ |
| **4** | **Connect Water Supply** | Attach supply lines to shut-off valves, replace valves if needed, verify hot/cold orientation | ☐ |
| **5** | **Reconnect Ancillaries & Prep for Test** | Reattach dishwasher, disposal, dispensers; final visual check | ☐ |

> ✅ **All 5 steps complete?** → Proceed to Section 10 — Testing & Commissioning.

---

### Detailed Steps

#### Step 1: Dry-Fit & Pre-Mount Assembly

**Dry-fit the new sink:**
1. Place new sink into cutout (drop-in) or hold in position (undermount) to verify fit
2. Check alignment with drain and supply rough-ins
3. Verify faucet holes align with selected faucet
4. Mark position if needed with painter's tape

**Install faucet on new sink (before mounting):**
> **Best practice:** Install faucet, strainer, and drain fittings on the sink before mounting — much easier access.

1. Insert faucet through mounting holes from above
2. Install rubber gasket and mounting hardware from below
3. Hand-tighten, then snug with basin wrench — **do not overtighten**
4. Connect supply line stubs to faucet inlets (hand-tight + 1/4 turn)

**Install basket strainer / drain fitting:**

*Kitchen sink (basket strainer):*
1. Roll plumber's putty into 1/2" rope
2. Apply putty ring around strainer flange
3. Insert strainer into drain hole from above
4. From below: install rubber gasket → cardboard friction ring → locknut
5. Tighten locknut while holding strainer from above to prevent rotation
6. Trim excess putty from above with putty knife

*Bathroom sink (pop-up drain):*
1. Apply plumber's putty or silicone (per manufacturer) to drain flange
2. Insert drain body from above
3. Thread drain body into tailpiece from below
4. Install pop-up linkage and adjust

#### Step 2: Mount & Seal Sink

**Drop-in (top-mount):**
1. Apply continuous bead of silicone sealant around countertop cutout edge
2. Carefully lower sink into cutout — align with marks
3. Press down firmly and evenly to seat
4. Install mounting clips from below — tighten evenly in star pattern
5. Wipe excess sealant from above and below

**Undermount:**
1. Apply silicone sealant to sink rim or countertop underside per manufacturer
2. Lift sink into position from below — **two-person operation**
3. Install mounting clips/brackets — tighten evenly
4. Support sink with temporary bracing until sealant cures (if required)
5. Clean excess sealant

**Wall-mount:**
1. Install wall bracket at correct height (31" – 34" AFF standard, or ADA height 34" max)
2. Verify bracket is level and secured to blocking/studs
3. Hang sink on bracket
4. Secure with mounting bolts

#### Step 3: Connect Drain System
1. Install tailpiece to strainer/drain body — hand-tight slip joint
2. Assemble P-trap:
   - Trap arm into wall stub-out (ensure proper slope — 1/4" per foot minimum)
   - P-trap bend connecting tailpiece to trap arm
3. Tighten all slip-joint nuts — **hand-tight plus 1/4 turn with pliers**
4. Ensure trap weir is not below the crown weir of the trap arm
5. Verify horizontal distance from trap weir to vent does not exceed code maximums

> ⚠️ **Code Check:** P-trap must be accessible, must have water seal of 2" – 4", and must not be double-trapped.

#### Step 4: Connect Water Supply
1. Connect braided stainless supply lines to shut-off valves
   - Hand-tight + 1/4 turn with wrench
   - **Do not use Teflon tape on compression fittings**
2. If replacing shut-off valves:
   - Cut supply pipe cleanly with tubing cutter
   - Deburr inside and outside
   - Install new 1/4-turn ball valve (compression or push-fit)
3. Verify hot is on left, cold is on right (facing the sink)

#### Step 5: Reconnect Ancillaries & Prep for Test
- Dishwasher drain hose to disposal or tailpiece air gap
- Filtered water line
- Soap dispenser
- Instant hot water dispenser
- Garbage disposal (if included in scope)

**Final visual check:**
- All connections hand-checked for tightness
- No kinks or stress on supply lines
- Work area clean and tools clear of cabinet

---

'''

# Reconstruct
new_content = content[:s8_start] + new_s8 + new_s9 + content[s10_start:]

with open(filepath, 'w') as f:
    f.write(new_content)

print('✅ Sections 8 and 9 fully rewritten with standardized format.')
print(f'File size: {len(new_content)} bytes')"
    }
}

| **4** | **Inspect & Prepare Surface** | Check for damage, rot, mold; clean all surfaces; clear drain pipe |
| **5** | **Document & Report** | Photograph conditions, report issues, get authorization |

### Section 9 — Installation

| Step | Step Name | Tasks |
|:---:|---|---|
| **1** | **Dry-Fit & Pre-Mount Assembly** | Verify sink fit, install faucet and drain fittings on bench |
| **2** | **Mount & Seal Sink** | Set sink into position, install clips, apply sealant, verify level |
| **3** | **Connect Drain System** | Install tailpiece, P-trap, trap arm; verify slope and code compliance |
| **4** | **Connect Water Supply** | Attach supply lines, replace valves if needed, verify hot/cold |
| **5** | **Reconnect Ancillaries & Prep for Test** | Reattach dishwasher, disposal, dispensers; final visual check |

---

**Changes made:**
- `Priority` → `Step`, `Group` → `Step Name` in both Executive Checklists
- `Tasks Covered` column removed from Section 8, replaced with `Tasks` (matching Section 9)
- Removed the 25-item detailed checklist table from Section 8 (matching Section 9)
- `Executive Checklist` and `Detailed Steps` are now proper `###` headings
- Step headings (`#### Step N:`) now use the **same group names** from the Executive Checklist
- Both sections are now identically structured and referenceable

This format is now ready to use as a **standardized template** for any trade SOP. 🔧

kvps.solutions:
# Problem
Create a standardized, professional Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for plumbing tasks (specifically sink replacement) optimized for digital work-order platforms and field use, featuring a strict two-tier checklist system.

# Solution
1. **SOP Structure & Documentation**: Use Markdown format (`.md`) with a consistent header: SOP Number, Trade, Task, Revision, Date, and Estimated Duration. Include Purpose, Scope (kitchen, bathroom, utility), and References (IPC/UPC/OSHA).
2. **Safety & Qualifications**: Detail Personnel Qualifications and Safety Requirements (PPE: glasses, gloves, knee pads) and a hazard mitigation table (water damage, sharp edges, electrical proximity).
3. **Tools & Materials**: Categorize essential items (basin wrenches, channel-locks, plumber's putty, silicone) and use callout blocks for safety warnings.
4. **Two-Tier Checklist System**: 
    - **Executive Checklist**: A high-level table with columns: 'Step', 'Step Name', 'Tasks', and '✓'. Group into 5 priority phases: 1. Isolate Services, 2. Disconnect/Remove, 3. Prepare/Inspect, 4. Install/Reconnect, 5. Document/Report.
    - **Detailed Steps**: Granular, numbered instructions (e.g., 'Rag stuffed into drain', 'On-bench assembly') using matching sub-headings (e.g., `#### Step 1: Isolate Services`) to provide technical depth.
5. **Standardized Formatting**: Ensure both removal and installation sections follow the hierarchy: `## [Section Number]. Procedure` -> `### Executive Checklist` -> `### Detailed Steps` -> `#### Step [N]: [Group Name]`.
6. **Testing & Commissioning**: Implement specific tests: Visual inspection, Pressure test (supply), Drain test (75% capacity), and the 'paper towel method' for leak detection.
7. **Quality & Closeout**: Include Cleanup, Documentation (photos, customer sign-off), and a Troubleshooting table for common issues (slow drains, water hammer).

# === 1. SOP AUTHORING GUIDE (the comprehensive prompt) ===

authoring_guide = r'''# SOP Authoring Guide
## Standard Operating Procedure — Creation & Management Manual

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Document ID** | SOP-META-001 |
| **Purpose** | Standardized creation of trade SOPs |
| **Revision** | 1.0 |
| **Effective Date** | 2026-02-28 |

---

## 1. Purpose

This guide defines the standardized format, structure, and conventions for authoring Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) across all trades. It ensures consistency, cross-referenceability, and usability for field workers, supervisors, and digital work-order platforms.

---

## 2. SOP Numbering Convention

### Format: `{TRADE}-SOP-{NNN}`

| Code | Trade | Example |
|---|---|---|
| PLB | Plumbing | PLB-SOP-001 |
| ELC | Electrical | ELC-SOP-001 |
| HVC | HVAC | HVC-SOP-001 |
| CRP | Carpentry | CRP-SOP-001 |
| PNT | Painting | PNT-SOP-001 |
| RFG | Roofing | RFG-SOP-001 |
| FLR | Flooring | FLR-SOP-001 |
| DRW | Drywall | DRW-SOP-001 |
| LND | Landscaping | LND-SOP-001 |
| GEN | General / Multi-trade | GEN-SOP-001 |
| MSN | Masonry | MSN-SOP-001 |
| WLD | Welding | WLD-SOP-001 |

Add new trade codes to the SOP Library Index (`SOP_INDEX.md`) as needed.

---

## 3. Mandatory Section Structure

Every SOP **must** contain the following sections in this order. Sections may be omitted only if explicitly marked N/A with justification.

```
# Standard Operating Procedure
## {Trade} — {Task Name}

| Header Table: SOP Number, Trade, Task, Revision, Date, Approved By, Est. Duration |

## 1. Purpose
## 2. Scope
## 3. References & Codes
## 4. Personnel & Qualifications
## 5. Safety Requirements
   ### 5.1 Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)
   ### 5.2 Hazard Awareness (table format)
   ### 5.3 Pre-Work Safety Checklist
## 6. Tools & Equipment
   ### 6.1 Required Tools (table format)
   ### 6.2 Required Materials (table format)
## 7. Pre-Work Assessment
## 8–N. Procedure Sections (one per phase — see Phase Rules below)
## N+1. Testing & Commissioning
## N+2. Cleanup & Closeout
## N+3. Troubleshooting (table format)
## N+4. Quality Standards
## N+5. Revision History (table format)
```

---

## 4. Procedure Phase Rules

Each procedure phase (e.g., Removal, Installation, Commissioning) gets its own numbered section and follows this **exact internal structure**:

```
## {N}. Procedure — {Phase Name}

### Executive Checklist

Complete each step in order. Check off the step when **all tasks within it are finished**.

| Step | Step Name | Tasks | ✓ |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| **1** | **{Step Name}** | {Brief task description} | ☐ |
| **2** | **{Step Name}** | {Brief task description} | ☐ |
| ... | ... | ... | ☐ |

> ✅ **All steps complete?** → Proceed to Section {next} — {next section name}.

---

### Detailed Steps

#### Step 1: {Step Name}
{Numbered instructions}

#### Step 2: {Step Name}
{Numbered instructions}

...
```

### Rules:

1. **Maximum 5 steps per Execut

<< 5260 Characters hidden >>

er specs)
   - Personnel & Qualifications (table: Role, Requirement)
   - Safety Requirements:
     - 5.1 PPE list
     - 5.2 Hazard Awareness table (columns: Hazard, Mitigation)
     - 5.3 Pre-Work Safety Checklist (checkbox format)
   - Tools & Equipment:
     - 6.1 Required Tools table (columns: Tool, Purpose)
     - 6.2 Required Materials table (columns: Material, Specification)
   - Pre-Work Assessment (site inspection, customer communication)

3. **Procedure Sections** — one per phase (e.g., Removal, Installation). Each MUST follow this structure:

   ```
   ## {N}. Procedure — {Phase Name}

   ### Executive Checklist
   | Step | Step Name | Tasks | ✓ |
   |:---:|---|---|:---:|
   | **1** | **{Name}** | {Brief description} | ☐ |
   (maximum 5 steps per checklist)

   ### Detailed Steps
   #### Step 1: {Same Step Name as Executive Checklist}
   {numbered instructions}
   ```

   Rules:
   - Maximum 5 steps per Executive Checklist
   - Step Names MUST match exactly between Executive Checklist and Detailed Steps headings
   - Use bold labels for sub-groupings within steps
   - Use `> ⚠️` for code checks and warnings
   - Use `> **Best practice:**` for recommendations

4. **Testing & Commissioning** — staged testing with checkbox format

5. **Cleanup & Closeout** — site cleanup, documentation, customer handoff

6. **Troubleshooting** table (columns: Issue, Probable Cause, Resolution)

7. **Quality Standards** — code compliance, workmanship standards

8. **Revision History** table (columns: Rev, Date, Author, Description)

**LINKING RULES:**
- Cross-reference: `(see {CODE}-SOP-{NNN}: {Title})`
- Prerequisite: `**Prerequisite:** Complete {CODE}-SOP-{NNN} before proceeding.`
- Sub-procedure: `**Sub-procedure:** Execute {CODE}-SOP-{NNN}: {Title} at this point. Return to Step {N} after completing.`
- Optional: `**If applicable:** See {CODE}-SOP-{NNN}: {Title}`

**RELATED SOPs** (for cross-referencing in Scope section):
{list any related SOPs that exist in the library}

**ADDITIONAL CONTEXT:**
{any trade-specific details, code requirements, or special considerations}
```

---

## 8. Quality Checklist for SOP Authors

Before publishing any SOP, verify:

- [ ] SOP Number follows `{TRADE}-SOP-{NNN}` convention
- [ ] All mandatory sections present in correct order
- [ ] Each procedure phase has Executive Checklist + Detailed Steps
- [ ] Executive Checklists have ≤ 5 steps each
- [ ] Step Names match exactly between Executive Checklist and Detailed Steps
- [ ] All cross-references use correct format and point to valid SOPs
- [ ] No circular references between SOPs
- [ ] Nesting depth ≤ 2 levels
- [ ] Safety section includes PPE, Hazard table, and Pre-Work checklist
- [ ] Tools and Materials tables are complete with specifications
- [ ] Testing section has staged verification steps
- [ ] Troubleshooting table covers common failure modes
- [ ] Revision History table is current
- [ ] SOP added to `SOP_INDEX.md` library
- [ ] File saved as `SOP_{Trade}_{Task_Name}.md` (underscores, no spaces)

---

## 9. Revision History

| Rev | Date | Author | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.0 | 2026-02-28 | — | Initial release |

---

*This guide is the authoritative reference for SOP creation. All SOPs must conform to this standard.*
'''

## Dependency Map

## Status Legend

## Adding a New SOP

1. Assign the next available number for the trade: `{TRADE}-SOP-{NNN}`
2. Author the SOP following `SOP_AUTHORING_GUIDE.md`

5. Update the **Quick Reference** count
6. Update the **Dependency Map** if the SOP links to others
7. Set status to 🔄 In Review until approved

---

## Revision History

| Rev | Date | Author | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.0 | 2026-02-28 | — | Initial release with PLB-SOP-001 |

*Keep this index current. Every SOP must have an entry here.*

### 📘 SOP_AUTHORING_GUIDE.md — What's inside:

| Section | Content |
|---|---|
| **Numbering Convention** | `{TRADE}-SOP-{NNN}` with 12 trade codes (PLB, ELC, HVC, CRP, etc.) |
| **Mandatory Structure** | Exact section order every SOP must follow |
| **Procedure Phase Rules** | Executive Checklist → Detailed Steps format with all rules (max 5 steps, name matching, formatting conventions) |
| **Linking & Nesting SOPs** | 5 link types with exact syntax, placement rules, and nesting limits |
| **AI Prompt Template** | Copy-paste prompt with `{placeholders}` to generate new SOPs |
| **Quality Checklist** | 15-point verification before publishing |

### 🔗 Linking & Nesting Rules (highlights):

| Link Type | Syntax |
|---|---|

| Sub-procedure | `**Sub-procedure:** Execute PLB-SOP-005 at this point. Return to Step N after completing.` |
| Optional | `**If applicable:** See PLB-SOP-006: Dishwasher Hookup` |
| Supersedes | `**Supersedes:** PLB-SOP-001 Rev 1.0` |

**Nesting rules:** Max depth 2, always specify return point, no circular references.

---

### 📊 To your question — **Yes, a library index table is essential.**

`SOP_INDEX.md` provides:

1. **Quick Reference** — SOP count per trade at a glance
2. **Full Registry Table** — Every SOP with: number, trade, task, revision, status, duration, file link, prerequisites, and sub-procedures
3. **Dependency Map** — ASCII diagram showing which SOPs invoke others
4. **Status Tracking** — ✅ Active, 📋 Planned, 🔄 In Review, ⚠️ Deprecated, ❌ Retired
5. **New SOP Checklist** — Steps to add a new SOP to the library

Without the index, you'd lose cross-reference integrity as the library grows. It's the single source of truth for "what SOPs exist, how they relate, and what's their status."

---

### 📝 SOP_TEMPLATE.md

Blank template with all `{placeholders}` — just fill in and go. Follows the exact format from the sink replacement SOP.

Want me to generate another trade SOP using the system (e.g., electrical outlet replacement, HVAC filter change) to validate the template? 🔧"
    }
}

SOP Authoring Guide (SOP-META-001) created to define trade codes (PLB, ELC, HVC, CRP, etc.), numbering conventions ({TRADE}-SOP-{NNN}), and mandatory section structures.

logs.heading: Solution memorization completed: 2 solutions processed, 2 intelligently consolidated
logs.content:
```json
[
  {
    "problem": "Create a standardized, professional Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for a plumbing trade task (sink replacement) with a specific hierarchical checklist structure.",
    "solution": "1. **Define SOP Structure**: Use a mandatory section order: Purpose, Scope, References/Codes, Personnel, Safety (PPE, Hazards, Checklist), Tools/Materials, Pre-Work, Procedure Phases, Testing, Cleanup, Troubleshooting, Quality Standards, and Revision History.
2. **Implement Two-Tier Checklist System**: For each procedure phase (e.g., Removal, Installation), create two distinct parts:
    - **Executive Checklist**: A high-level table with columns `Step`, `Step Name`, `Tasks`, and `✓`. Limit to a maximum of 5 grouped steps for quick supervisor/field oversight.
    - **Detailed Steps**: Use `#### Step N: [Step Name]` headings that match the Executive Checklist exactly. Provide granular, numbered instructions under each heading.
3. **Standardize Formatting**: Use bold labels for sub-groupings (e.g., **Supply lines:**), blockquotes for code checks (`> ⚠️ **Code Check:**`), and checkboxes (`☐`) for all verification items.
4. **Apply to Sink Replacement**: 
    - **Removal Phase**: Steps include Isolate Services, Disconnect Plumbing, Remove Fixtures, Inspect Surface, and Document/Report.
    - **Installation Phase**: Steps include Dry-Fit/Pre-Mount, Mount/Seal Sink, Connect Drain, Connect Water Supply, and Reconnect Ancillaries."
  },
  {
    "problem": "Establish a system for managing a library of trade SOPs, including authoring guidelines, templates, and cross-referencing rules.",
    "solution": "1. **Create an Authoring Guide**: Define a naming convention (e.g., `{TRADE}-SOP-{NNN}`) and provide an AI prompt template that enforces the standardized structure (Executive Checklist + Detailed Steps).
2. **Implement Linking and Nesting Rules**: 
    - **Cross-reference**: Use `(see {CODE}-SOP-{NNN}: {Title})` for related tasks.
    - **Prerequisite**: Use `**Prerequisite:** Complete {SOP-ID} before proceeding.`
    - **Sub-procedure**: Use `**Sub-procedure:** Execute {SOP-ID} at this point. Return to Step {N} after completing.`
    - **Nesting Limit**: Restrict nesting to a maximum depth of 2 levels to prevent complexity and circular references.
3. **Maintain a Library Index (`SOP_INDEX.md`)**: Create a master registry table containing SOP Number, Trade, Task, Revision, Status (Active, Planned, In Review), File Link, and Dependencies.
4. **Include a Dependency Map**: Use an ASCII or visual diagram in the index to show how SOPs relate (e.g., Sink Replacement → Garbage Disposal Install)."
  }
]
```

kvps.solutions:
# Problem
Establish a comprehensive system for managing a library of trade Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs), including standardized authoring, hierarchical structures, and cross-referencing rules for field and digital use.

# Solution
1. **Standardized SOP Structure**: Use a mandatory section order: Purpose, Scope, References/Codes, Personnel Qualifications, Safety (PPE/Hazards), Tools/Materials, Pre-Work, Procedure Phases, Testing, Cleanup, Troubleshooting, and Revision History.
2. **Two-Tier Checklist System**: For each procedure phase, implement:
    - **Executive Checklist**: A high-level table (Step, Name, Tasks, ✓) limited to ~5 grouped steps for quick oversight.
    - **Detailed Steps**: Granular, numbered instructions matching the Executive Checklist headings.
3. **Authoring & Naming Conventions**: Define a naming convention (e.g., `{TRADE}-SOP-{NNN}`) and use AI prompt templates to enforce the standardized structure and Markdown formatting (bold labels, blockquotes for code checks, callout blocks for safety).
4. **Linking and Nesting Rules**: 
    - **Cross-reference**: Use `(see {CODE}-SOP-{NNN}: {Title})`.
    - **Prerequisite**: Use `**Prerequisite:** Complete {SOP-ID} before proceeding.`
    - **Sub-procedure**: Use `**Sub-procedure:** Execute {SOP-ID}. Return to Step {N} after completing.`
    - **Nesting Limit**: Restrict nesting to a maximum depth of 2 levels to prevent circular references.
5. **Library Management**: Maintain a master index (`SOP_INDEX.md`) containing SOP Number, Trade, Task, Revision, Status, and Dependencies. Include a Dependency Map (ASCII/visual) to show procedural flow (e.g., Sink Replacement → Garbage Disposal Install).
6. **Testing & Quality**: Implement specific validation methods (e.g., 'paper towel method' for leaks) and documentation requirements (photos, customer sign-off) for closeout.

# Standard Operating Procedure
## Electrical — Outlet Replacement

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| **SOP Number** | ELC-SOP-001 |
| **Trade** | Electrical |
| **Task** | Remove & Replace Electrical Outlet (Receptacle) — 120V/240V Residential & Light-Commercial |
| **Revision** | 1.0 |
| **Effective Date** | 2026-02-28 |
| **Approved By** | _________________________ |
| **Estimated Duration** | 30 min – 2 hours (varies by scope and circuit complexity) |

---

## 1. Purpose

This procedure defines the safe, code-compliant steps for removing an existing electrical outlet (receptacle) and installing a replacement. It covers standard duplex receptacles, GFCI receptacles, USB-equipped receptacles, and 240V receptacles in residential and light-commercial settings.

---

## 2. Scope

This SOP covers:
- De-energizing the circuit and verifying zero-energy state
- Removal of the existing receptacle, cover plate, and wiring connections
- Installation of the replacement receptacle with proper wiring terminations
- Testing for correct wiring, grounding, and GFCI protection
- Cover plate installation and final commissioning

This SOP does **not** cover:
- New circuit installation or home run wiring (see ELC-SOP-005: New Branch Circuit Installation)
- Electrical panel upgrades or breaker replacement (see ELC-SOP-010: Panel & Breaker Service)
- Whole-room rewiring or knob-and-tube replacement (see ELC-SOP-015: Legacy Wiring Remediation)
- Dedicated 240V circuit installation for appliances (see ELC-SOP-020: Dedicated Appliance Circuit)
- Smart outlet / home automation integration (see ELC-SOP-025: Smart Device Installation)
- Outdoor / wet-location receptacle installation requiring new weatherproof boxes (see ELC-SOP-030: Exterior Receptacle Installation)

---

## 3. References & Codes

- National Electrical Code (NEC / NFPA 70) — current edition
- Local jurisdiction amendments and permit requirements
- Manufacturer installation instructions for specific receptacle models
- OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart K — Electrical (Construction)
- OSHA 29 CFR 1910 Subpart S — Electrical (General Industry)
- NFPA 70E — Standard for Electrical Safety in the Workplace

---

## 4. Personnel & Qualifications

| Role | Requirement |
|---|---|
| Lead Electrician | Licensed journeyman electrician or equivalent per jurisdiction |
| Apprentice (if assisting) | Enrolled electrical apprentice under direct supervision |
| Inspector (if required) | Local AHJ electrical inspector for final sign-off |

---

## 5. Safety Requirements

### 5.1 Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)
- Safety glasses / goggles
- Insulated electrical gloves (rated for voltage class — minimum Class 00 for 120V)
- Leather protector gloves over insulated gloves
- Non-conductive / dielectric footwear
- Arc-rated clothing (if panel work required — minimum 8 cal/cm²)
- Hearing protection (if using power tools)

### 5.2 Hazard Awareness
| Hazard | Mitigation |
|---|---|
| Electrical shock (120V/240V) | LOTO procedure; verify zero-energy with rated tester; never trust a single test |
| Arc flash | Wear arc-rated PPE; maintain safe approach boundaries per NFPA 70E |
| Fire risk | Verify wire gauge matches breaker rating; ensure tight connections; no backstab on 20A circuits |
| Damaged / deteriorated wiring | Inspect all visible wi

<< 11259 Characters hidden >>

| ☐ |

> ✅ **All 5 steps complete?** → Proceed to Section 10 — Testing & Commissioning.

---

### Detailed Steps

#### Step 1: Prepare & Wire the Receptacle

**Verify device before wiring:**
1. Confirm replacement receptacle matches requirements:
   - Amperage: 15A or 20A (must match or be compatible with circuit)
   - Voltage: 120V or 240V
   - Type: Standard duplex, GFCI, decorator, tamper-resistant (TR), weather-resistant (WR)
   - Rating: UL listed; CO/ALR if aluminum wiring
2. Inspect new device for shipping damage

**Wire termination — standard duplex receptacle:**
1. Form a clockwise hook ("shepherd's hook") on each conductor end using needle-nose pliers
2. Connect **GROUND** (bare/green) to the green screw terminal first
   - If metal box: also bond ground to box with ground screw or clip
3. Connect **NEUTRAL** (white) to the silver screw terminal
   - Hook wraps clockwise under screw head
   - Tighten screw firmly — wire should not pull free with gentle tug
4. Connect **HOT** (black) to the brass screw terminal
   - Hook wraps clockwise under screw head
   - Tighten screw firmly
5. If daisy-chaining (multiple cables): use pigtails to a single connection point rather than using both sets of device terminals

**Wire termination — GFCI receptacle:**
1. Identify LINE cable (from panel) and LOAD cable (to downstream outlets)
2. Connect LINE hot (black) to **LINE brass** terminal (marked on device)
3. Connect LINE neutral (white) to **LINE silver** terminal
4. Connect LOAD hot (black) to **LOAD brass** terminal (if protecting downstream)
5. Connect LOAD neutral (white) to **LOAD silver** terminal
6. Connect ground to green screw
7. If no downstream protection needed: cap LOAD terminals with provided stickers or leave LOAD terminals unused

> ⚠️ **CRITICAL:** Reversing LINE and LOAD on a GFCI will prevent it from protecting downstream outlets and may prevent the device from functioning. Always verify with manufacturer markings.

**Wire termination — 240V receptacle:**
1. Connect both hot conductors (black and red) to brass terminals
2. Connect neutral (white) to silver terminal (if 4-wire / NEMA 14)
3. Connect ground (bare/green) to green terminal
4. Verify wire gauge matches breaker and receptacle rating

> **Best practice:** Use screw-to-clamp terminals when available — they provide more reliable connections than backstab (push-in) and are faster than hook-and-screw. Never use backstab connections on 20A circuits or with 12 AWG wire.

#### Step 2: Secure Device in Box
1. Carefully fold wires in an accordion pattern into the box
   - **Do not force or kink wires** — especially critical with aluminum
   - Ground wire goes to the back first, then neutral, then hot
2. Align receptacle mounting ears with box screw holes
3. Insert and tighten top mounting screw — snug, not overtightened
4. Insert and tighten bottom mounting screw
5. Check plumb with torpedo level — adjust using mounting screw slots
6. Verify receptacle sits flush with wall surface
7. Verify no wire insulation is pinched between device and box

#### Step 3: Install Cover Plate
1. Position cover plate over receptacle
2. Insert cover plate screw(s) — hand-tighten, then snug with screwdriver
3. **Do not overtighten** — cover plates crack easily (especially nylon/plastic)
4. Verify cover plate sits flush against wall with no gaps
5. If wall surface is uneven: use a gasketed or oversized cover plate

#### Step 4: Energize & Test
1. Return to electrical panel
2. Remove lockout/tagout device
3. Turn breaker to ON position
4. Return to receptacle
5. **Test with receptacle tester (3-light or digital):**
   - Verify correct wiring indication (two amber lights for standard 120V)
   - Check for open ground, open neutral, hot/neutral reverse, hot/ground reverse
6. **Test with rated voltage tester:**
   - Hot to neutral: 120V (±5%) for standard; 240V (±5%) for 240V receptacle
   - Hot to ground: 120V (±5%)
   - Neutral to ground: <2V (higher indicates neutral/ground issue)
7. **Test GFCI function (if GFCI receptacle):**
   - Press TEST button — receptacle should trip (power off)
   - Press RESET button — receptacle should restore power
   - Test with external GFCI tester — verify trip time <25ms
   - Verify downstream outlets also lose power when GFCI trips (if LOAD terminals used)

#### Step 5: Commission & Document
1. Verify all downstream outlets on the circuit are functioning
2. Plug in a load (lamp, phone charger) to confirm real-world operation
3. Label the circuit at the electrical panel if not already labeled
4. Apply GFCI sticker to downstream outlets protected by upstream GFCI ("GFCI Protected" label per NEC 406.4(D))
5. Complete work order documentation

---

## 10. Testing & Commissioning

### Step 1: Visual Inspection
- [ ] Receptacle is plumb and flush with wall
- [ ] Cover plate is secure with no gaps
- [ ] No visible wire insulation outside the box
- [ ] Correct receptacle type installed for location (GFCI, TR, WR as required)
- [ ] Receptacle amperage matches circuit breaker rating

### Step 2: Electrical Test — Receptacle Tester
- [ ] 3-light / digital tester shows correct wiring
- [ ] No fault conditions indicated (open ground, reverse polarity, etc.)

### Step 3: Electrical Test — Voltage Verification
- [ ] Hot-to-neutral voltage: within spec (114V–126V for 120V nominal)
- [ ] Hot-to-ground voltage: within spec
- [ ] Neutral-to-ground voltage: <2V
- [ ] Both outlets tested (top and bottom of duplex)

### Step 4: GFCI Verification (if applicable)
- [ ] TEST button trips the device
- [ ] RESET button restores power
- [ ] External GFCI tester confirms trip
- [ ] Downstream outlets lose power when GFCI trips
- [ ] Downstream outlets labeled "GFCI Protected"

### Step 5: Load Test
- [ ] Device powers a real load (lamp, tool, appliance) without flickering or heat
- [ ] No buzzing, arcing sounds, or burning smell
- [ ] After 5 minutes under load: touch cover plate — should not be warm
- [ ] Plug fits snugly — not loose in receptacle slots

### Step 6: Final Safety Verification
- [ ] Breaker labeled correctly at panel
- [ ] LOTO device removed from panel
- [ ] All tools and materials removed from work area
- [ ] **Zero deficiencies confirmed** ✓

---

## 11. Cleanup & Closeout

### Cleanup
1. Remove all debris, wire scraps, old receptacle, and packaging
2. Clean wall area around cover plate (remove fingerprints, dust)
3. Vacuum / sweep work area
4. Dispose of old receptacle properly (e-waste if applicable)

### Documentation
1. Complete work order with:
   - Actual time spent (start/end)
   - Materials used (receptacle model, wire nuts, pigtails)
   - Photos: before, during (wiring), after
   - Any additional work performed or recommended
   - Wire type identified (copper/aluminum)
   - Test results (voltage readings, GFCI trip confirmation)
2. Note any code concerns or recommended follow-up work:
   - Other outlets on circuit needing upgrade
   - Panel labeling corrections
   - Aluminum wiring remediation recommendations
3. Obtain customer sign-off on completed work

### Customer Handoff
- Demonstrate receptacle operation
- Show GFCI test/reset procedure (if applicable)
- Explain tamper-resistant feature (if customer is unfamiliar)
- Identify circuit breaker location and label
- Advise on monthly GFCI testing requirement
- Provide manufacturer warranty information
- Leave contact information for warranty service calls

---

## 12. Troubleshooting

| Issue | Probable Cause | Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| Receptacle tester shows open ground | Ground wire not connected; broken ground path | Reconnect ground; check continuity back to panel |
| Receptacle tester shows hot/neutral reverse | Hot and neutral wires swapped at terminals | Swap wires to correct terminals (black→brass, white→silver) |
| Receptacle tester shows open neutral | Neutral wire not connected or broken | Check neutral connection; inspect for broken wire in box |
| GFCI won't reset | No power to LINE terminals; defective device; LINE/LOAD reversed | Verify power at LINE terminals; check wiring; replace if defective |
| GFCI trips immediately | Ground fault on LOAD side; neutral-ground bond downstream | Disconnect LOAD wires and reset; isolate fault on downstream circuit |
| Outlet is warm to touch | Loose connection; wire gauge undersized; backstab connection failing | Re-terminate all connections with screw terminals; verify wire gauge |
| Intermittent power | Loose terminal connection; backstab connection; damaged wire | Re-strip and re-terminate; convert backstab to screw terminal |
| Buzzing or arcing sound | Loose connection; damaged receptacle; arcing at terminal | De-energize immediately; inspect and re-terminate or replace device |
| Breaker trips when outlet is used | Short circuit; ground fault; overloaded circuit | Inspect wiring for shorts; check load calculation; isolate fault |
| Plug falls out of receptacle | Worn contact springs in receptacle | Replace receptacle — contacts are not repairable |
| Sparking when plugging in | Normal if brief; abnormal if sustained | Brief spark is normal (inrush current); sustained spark = loose connection or damaged device |

---

## 13. Quality Standards

- All work shall comply with the National Electrical Code (NEC/NFPA 70) and local amendments
- All materials shall be new, UL-listed, and rated for the intended use
- All wire terminations shall be mechanically secure with no exposed copper outside the device
- Screw terminal connections preferred over backstab (push-in) for reliability
- GFCI protection shall be provided at all code-required locations
- All replacement receptacles shall be tamper-resistant (TR) per NEC 406.12
- Receptacle amperage shall match or be compatible with circuit breaker and wire gauge
- Workmanship shall be neat, professional, and pass visual inspection
- Customer property shall be protected and left clean
- Circuit shall be properly labeled at the electrical panel

---

## 14. Revision History

| Rev | Date | Author | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.0 | 2026-02-28 | — | Initial release |

---

*This SOP is a living document. Report any errors, omissions, or improvement suggestions to the Operations Manager.*
SOPEOF

| Outlet is warm to touch | Loose connection; wire gauge undersized; backstab connection failing | Re-terminate all connections with screw terminals; verify wire gauge |
| Intermittent power | Loose terminal connection; backstab connection; damaged wire | Re-strip and re-terminate; convert backstab to screw terminal |
| Buzzing or arcing sound | Loose connection; damaged receptacle; arcing at terminal | De-energize immediately; inspect and re-terminate or replace device |
| Breaker trips when outlet is used | Short circuit; ground fault; overloaded circuit | Inspect wiring for shorts; check load calculation; isolate fault |
| Plug falls out of receptacle | Worn contact springs in receptacle | Replace receptacle — contacts are not repairable |
| Sparking when plugging in | Normal if brief; abnormal if sustained | Brief spark is normal (inrush current); sustained spark = loose connection or damaged device |

---

## 13. Quality Standards

- All work shall comply with the National Electrical Code (NEC/NFPA 70) and local amendments
- All materials shall be new, UL-listed, and rated for the intended use
- All wire terminations shall be mechanically secure with no exposed copper outside the device
- Screw terminal connections preferred over backstab (push-in) for reliability
- GFCI protection shall be provided at all code-required locations
- All replacement receptacles shall be tamper-resistant (TR) per NEC 406.12
- Receptacle amperage shall match or be compatible with circuit breaker and wire gauge
- Workmanship shall be neat, professional, and pass visual inspection
- Customer property shall be protected and left clean
- Circuit shall be properly labeled at the electrical panel

---

## 14. Revision History

| Rev | Date | Author | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.0 | 2026-02-28 | — | Initial release |

---

*This SOP is a living document. Report any errors, omissions, or improvement suggestions to the Operations Manager.*
SOPEOF
echo "SOP created successfully."

kvps.memories:
The AI assistant established a standardized format for Trade Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) consisting of an Executive Checklist (max 5 steps) followed by Detailed Steps with matching headings.

SOPs follow a mandatory section structure: Purpose, Scope, References & Codes, Personnel & Qualifications, Safety Requirements (PPE, Hazard table, Pre-Work checklist), Tools & Equipment, Pre-Work Assessment, Procedure Phases, Testing & Commissioning, Cleanup & Closeout, Troubleshooting, Quality Standards, and Revision History.

A standardized SOP numbering convention was created using the format {TRADE}-SOP-{NNN} (e.g., PLB-SOP-001 for Plumbing, ELC-SOP-001 for Electrical).

Specific linking and nesting rules for SOPs were defined: Cross-references (see CODE-SOP-NNN), Prerequisites, Sub-procedures (with return points), and Optional add-ons, with a maximum nesting depth of 2 levels.

A master library index (SOP_INDEX.md) is required to track all SOPs, their status (Active, Planned, In Review, Deprecated, Retired), and their dependencies.

The project directory contains 'SOP_Plumbing_Sink_Replacement.md' (PLB-SOP-001) and 'SOP_Electrical_Outlet_Replacement.md' (ELC-SOP-001) as reference implementations.

Authoring resources created include 'SOP_AUTHORING_GUIDE.md' (containing a comprehensive AI prompt for generating new SOPs) and 'SOP_TEMPLATE.md' (a blank fill-in-the-blanks Markdown file).

logs.heading: Solution memorization completed: 2 solutions processed, 2 intelligently consolidated
logs.content:
```json
[
  {
    "problem": "Create a standardized, trade-ready Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) format for technical tasks (e.g., plumbing, electrical) that includes executive summaries and detailed instructions.",
    "solution": "1. **Define a Standardized Structure**: Every SOP must include: Header Table (ID, Trade, Task, Rev, Date), Purpose, Scope (In/Out), References & Codes, Personnel & Qualifications, Safety Requirements (PPE, Hazard Table, Pre-Work Checklist), Tools & Materials Tables, Pre-Work Assessment, Procedure Phases, Testing & Commissioning, Cleanup & Closeout, Troubleshooting Table, and Quality Standards.
2. **Implement a Two-Tier Procedure Format**: Each procedure phase (e.g., Removal, Installation) must have:
    - **Executive Checklist**: A table with columns [Step, Step Name, Tasks, ✓]. Limit to maximum 5 steps. Step Names must match the detailed headings exactly.
    - **Detailed Steps**: Numbered instructions under `#### Step N: [Step Name]` headings. Use bold labels for sub-groupings (e.g., **Supply lines:**) and blockquotes for warnings (`> ⚠️ **Code Check:**`) or best practices (`> **Best practice:**`).
3. **Establish Linking & Nesting Rules**:
    - **Cross-reference**: `(see {TRADE}-SOP-{NNN}: {Title})`
    - **Prerequisite**: `**Prerequisite:** Complete {SOP-ID} before proceeding.`
    - **Sub-procedure**: `**Sub-procedure:** Execute {SOP-ID} at this point. Return to Step {N} after completing.`
    - **Nesting Limit**: Maximum depth of 2 levels to prevent circular references and complexity.
4. **Maintain a Library Index (`SOP_INDEX.md`)**: Create a master catalog containing a Quick Reference count per trade, a Full Registry Table (SOP ID, Task, Status, File Link, Dependencies), and a Dependency Map (ASCII tree) showing how SOPs invoke each other."
  },
  {
    "problem": "Standardize an existing plumbing SOP to follow a new executive/detailed two-tier format.",
    "solution": "1. **Identify Phases**: Group the existing 25-36 individual tasks into 5 logical 'Executive' steps (e.g., Isolate Services, Disconnect, Remove, Inspect, Document).
2. **Update Headings**: Rename 'Priority' to 'Step' and 'Group' to 'Step Name' in the summary table.
3. **Sync Headings**: Ensure the detailed instruction headings (e.g., `#### Step 1: Isolate Services`) match the 'Step Name' in the table exactly.
4. **Consolidate**: Move the brief task descriptions into a 'Tasks' column in the Executive Checklist and keep the granular numbered lists in the 'Detailed Steps' section below."
  }
]
```

kvps.solutions:
{'problem': 'Create a standardized, trade-ready Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) format for technical tasks (e.g., plumbing, electrical) that includes executive summaries and detailed instructions.', 'solution': '1. **Define a Standardized Structure**: Every SOP must include: Header Table (ID, Trade, Task, Rev, Date), Purpose, Scope (In/Out), References & Codes, Personnel & Qualifications, Safety Requirements (PPE, Hazard Table, Pre-Work Checklist), Tools & Materials Tables, Pre-Work Assessment, Procedure Phases, Testing & Commissioning, Cleanup & Closeout, Troubleshooting Table, and Quality Standards.
2. **Implement a Two-Tier Procedure Format**: Each procedure phase (e.g., Removal, Installation) must have:
    - **Executive Checklist**: A table with columns [Step, Step Name, Tasks, ✓]. Limit to maximum 5 steps. Step Names must match the detailed headings exactly.
    - **Detailed Steps**: Numbered instructions under `#### Step N: [Step Name]` headings. Use bold labels for sub-groupings (e.g., **Supply lines:**) and blockquotes for warnings (`> ⚠️ **Code Check:**`) or best practices (`> **Best practice:**`).
3. **Establish Linking & Nesting Rules**:
    - **Cross-reference**: `(see {TRADE}-SOP-{NNN}: {Title})`
    - **Prerequisite**: `**Prerequisite:** Complete {SOP-ID} before proceeding.`
    - **Sub-procedure**: `**Sub-procedure:** Execute {SOP-ID} at this point. Return to Step {N} after completing.`
    - **Nesting Limit**: Maximum depth of 2 levels to prevent circular references and complexity.
4. **Maintain a Library Index (`SOP_INDEX.md`)**: Create a master catalog containing a Quick Reference count per trade, a Full Registry Table (SOP ID, Task, Status, File Link, Dependencies), and a Dependency Map (ASCII tree) showing how SOPs invoke each other.'}

{'problem': 'Standardize an existing plumbing SOP to follow a new executive/detailed two-tier format.', 'solution': "1. **Identify Phases**: Group the existing 25-36 individual tasks into 5 logical 'Executive' steps (e.g., Isolate Services, Disconnect, Remove, Inspect, Document).
2. **Update Headings**: Rename 'Priority' to 'Step' and 'Group' to 'Step Name' in the summary table.
3. **Sync Headings**: Ensure the detailed instruction headings (e.g., `#### Step 1: Isolate Services`) match the 'Step Name' in the table exactly.
4. **Consolidate**: Move the brief task descriptions into a 'Tasks' column in the Executive Checklist and keep the granular numbered lists in the 'Detailed Steps' section below."}

kvps.memories:
SOP Authoring Guide (SOP-META-001): Standards for Trade SOP Creation.

Resources:
- SOP_AUTHORING_GUIDE.md: Contains a comprehensive AI prompt for generating new SOPs based on these standards.
- SOP_TEMPLATE.md: A blank fill-in-the-blanks Markdown file following the mandatory structure.

1. Identification & Metadata:
- Trade Codes: PLB (Plumbing), ELC (Electrical), HVC (HVAC), CRP (Carpentry), etc.
- Numbering Convention: {TRADE}-SOP-{NNN} (e.g., PLB-SOP-001, ELC-SOP-001).

2. Mandatory Document Structure:
Purpose, Scope, References & Codes, Personnel & Qualifications, Safety Requirements (PPE, Hazard table, Pre-Work checklist), Tools & Equipment, Pre-Work Assessment, Procedure Phases, Testing & Commissioning, Cleanup & Closeout, Troubleshooting, Quality Standards, and Revision History.

3. Procedure Phase Formatting:
- Executive Checklist: Heading with table (Step, Step Name, Tasks, ✓). Max 5 steps per checklist.
- Detailed Steps: Heading with sub-steps (#### Step N: [Step Name]).
- Mapping Integrity: Step names must match exactly between the Executive Checklist and detailed instructions.

4. Linking & Cross-Reference Rules:
- Cross-references: (see CODE-SOP-NNN)
- Prerequisites: (Complete {ID} before)
- Sub-procedures: (Execute {ID} at this point; requires specified return point)
- Optional Add-ons: (If applicable, see {ID})
- Nesting Constraints: Max depth of 2 levels; circular references prohibited.

# Insert before Testing & Commissioning
old_testing = '## {N}. Testing & Commissioning'
content = content.replace(old_testing, video_template + '## {N+2}. Testing & Commissioning')

# Update subsequent section numbers
content = content.replace('## {N+1}. Cleanup & Closeout', '## {N+3}. Cleanup & Closeout')
content = content.replace('## {N+2}. Troubleshooting', '## {N+4}. Troubleshooting')
content = content.replace('## {N+3}. Quality Standards', '## {N+5}. Quality Standards')
content = content.replace('## {N+4}. Revision History', '## {N+6}. Revision History')

with open(fpath, 'w') as f:
    f.write(content)
print(f'✅ SOP_TEMPLATE.md updated ({len(content):,} bytes)')

print('\n--- Template and Guide updated. Now updating existing SOPs... ---')

kvps.memories:
The AI assistant established a standardized system for creating and managing trade-specific Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs), stored in the project directory under `docs/general/`.

The SOP system consists of four core documents: `SOP_AUTHORING_GUIDE.md` (rules and AI prompts), `SOP_INDEX.md` (library catalog and dependency map), `SOP_TEMPLATE.md` (blank fillable format), and individual trade SOPs like `SOP_Plumbing_Sink_Replacement.md` and `SOP_Electrical_Outlet_Replacement.md`.

Standardized SOP numbering follows the format `{TRADE}-SOP-{NNN}` (e.g., PLB-SOP-001 for Plumbing, ELC-SOP-001 for Electrical).

Every SOP follows a mandatory 15-section structure: 1. Purpose, 2. Scope, 3. References & Codes, 4. Personnel, 5. Safety, 6. Tools & Equipment, 7. Pre-Work Assessment, 8. Procedure—Removal, 9. Procedure—Installation, 10. Recommended Videos, 11. Testing & Commissioning, 12. Cleanup & Closeout, 13. Troubleshooting, 14. Quality Standards, and 15. Revision History.

Procedure sections (8 and 9) utilize a three-tier structure: an Executive Checklist (max 5 steps for high-level tracking), Detailed Steps (granular numbered instructions with matching headings), and Recommended Videos (curated YouTube links demonstrating the specific steps).

SOP linking and nesting rules allow for cross-references, prerequisites, and sub-procedures (e.g., a sink install SOP calling a garbage disposal sub-procedure), with a maximum nesting depth of 2 levels and a prohibition on circular references.

The 'Recommended Videos' section (Section 10) is mandatory for all SOPs, organized by procedure phase, and must include the video title, URL, duration, and source (preferring official or manufacturer channels).

The `SOP_INDEX.md` serves as the master registry, tracking the status (Active, Planned, In Review, Deprecated, Retired), revision, and dependencies of all SOPs in the library.
